The deity was identified as '''{{ill|Ziwei Emperor|lt=Beiji Ziwei Dadi|ja|北極紫微大帝}}'''. This belief later syncretized with the {{ill|Big Dipper Faith|ja|北斗信仰}}, deifying the Big Dipper as '''{{ill|Beidou Zhenjun|ja|北斗星君}}'''. In time, it further merged with the Buddhist concept of {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}, forming the '''Myōken faith'''. Consequently, "Beiji Ziwei Dadi" (North Pole Purple Tenuous Emperor) and "Beidou Zhenjun" (Big Dipper True Lord) are sometimes considered distinct and sometimes identified as the same deity.
The belief in the North Star's divine power extended to Chinese emperors and Japan’s imperial family. The robes of Chinese emperors featured the {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星}} on the left sleeve and the {{ill|Zhinü|lt=Weaving Maiden|ja|織女}}<ref group="note">This does not refer to {{ill|Vega|ja|ベガ}} alone but to the constellation {{ill|Weaving Maiden Three Stars|ja|織女三星}}, which includes Vega.</ref> on the right. Meanwhile, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} preserves an imperial robe of {{ill|Emperor Komei|ja|孝明天皇}}, with the Big Dipper embroidered at the center of the upper back, though without the Weaving Maiden.
== The Heavenly Sovereign Emperor as a Constellation == [[File:和漢三才図絵「北極紫微垣之図」部分・上下逆.JPG|thumb|230px|right|''{{ill|Wakan Sansai Zue|ja|和漢三才図会}}'': "Map of the Purple Forbidden Enclosure" with ''Beiji Ziwei Dadi'' (北極紫微大帝) and ''Heavenly Sovereign Emperor'' (天皇大帝)]]
Beyond its religious significance, "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor" also referred to an actual asterism. It is mentioned in ''{{ill|Kaiyuan Zhanjing|ja|開元占経}}'', Volume 69, "Ganshi's Central Observations" (甘氏中官占)<ref>Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 48.</ref>. In the ''{{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}'', ''Tianwen Zhi'' (Astronomical Records) states: "Within the 'mouth' is one star called 'Heavenly Sovereign Emperor'" (口中一星を天皇大帝と曰ふ).
This "mouth" refers to the asterism ''Gouchen'' (勾陳), which guarded the celestial palace of the Jade Emperor, the {{ill|Purple Forbidden Enclosure|ja|紫微垣}}<ref>Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 153.</ref>. The star known as "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor" is believed to be {{ill|HD212710|ja|HD212710}}, a 5th magnitude star in {{ill|Cepheus|ja|ケフェウス座}}<ref group="note">Modern identifications were made using the star catalog data from ''Imperial Astronomical Treatise'' (欽定儀象考成) by Tsuchihashi, Chevalier, and Iida. See Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 297.</ref>.
== Worship and Rituals == The {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星}} was seen as a celestial ladle pouring cosmic blessings. The ''{{ill|Records of the Grand Historian|ja|史記}}'' describes the Big Dipper as revolving around the North Star, marking time, seasons, and ensuring the stability of the state<ref>''Shiji'' "Treatise on Astronomy" and ''Star Canon'' (星経), "The Big Dipper" section.</ref>.
The Heavenly Sovereign Emperor is associated with divine regalia, specifically a '''mirror''' and a '''sword'''. These items serve as both ritual implements and symbols of authority.
== Reading and Pronunciation == According to {{ill|Tetsuji Morohashi|ja|諸橋轍次}}’s ''{{ill|Dai Kan-Wa Jiten|ja|大漢和辞典}}'' (1956), the historical reading of "天皇大帝" was ''Ten'wō Taitei'' (てんわうだいてい), pronounced as ''Tennō Daitei'' due to {{ill|Rendaku|ja|連声}}. As a constellation name, it was commonly read as ''Tenkō Taitei'' (てんこうたいてい)<ref>Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 173.</ref>.== See Also == * {{ill|North Star|ja|北極星}} * {{ill|Northern Celestial Pole|ja|北辰}} * {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}} * [[:en:Ziwei_enclosure|purple forbidden enclosure]]
[[Category:Taoism]] [[Category:Chinese constellations]] [[Category:Four Sovereigns]] [[ja:天皇大帝]]
[[Category:Completion tier 1]][[Category: tier 1]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tianhuang Emperor]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tianhuang Emperor]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Taoism]] [[Category:Chinese constellations]] [[Category:Four heavenly ministers]] [[Category:Expanded pages from Wikipedia]][[en:Tianhuang Emperor]] [[ja:天皇大帝]] ==Notes== <references group="note"/>
==References== {{DEFAULTSORT:Tianhuang Emperor}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tianhuang Emperor}} [[Category:New]]

{{for|The figure given the posthumous title of "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor"|{{ill|Liu Yan (Southern Han)|ja|劉龑}}}} [[File:天皇大帝.JPG|thumb|Statue of the Heavenly Sovereign Emperor (Tianhuang Dadi) at Okayamajutenkyu, Taiwan]] [[File:Sterneamwalberla2.jpg|thumb|230px|right|Stars rotating around the North Star]] [[File:Draco constellation map.png|thumb|230px|right|Ursa Minor and Draco. The current North Star is marked as "Polaris"]]
'''Heavenly Sovereign Emperor''' (天皇大帝, ''Ten'nō Taitei''<ref>{{Cite book|author=Saburōsuke Tanabe|year=1991|title=Comprehensive Encyclopedia of Japanese Buddhist Statues, Volume 5: Syncretic Deities and Eminent Monks|page=18|publisher={{ill|Gyosei|ja|ぎょうせい}}|isbn=4-324-02117-1}}</ref>) refers to the deification of the '''Northern Celestial Pole''' (北辰, ''Hokushin'')<ref>Hisao Fukushima, ''The Night Sky as Seen by Confucius,'' {{ill|Taishukan Shoten|ja|大修館書店}}, 1997.</ref>. It is considered one of the most prominent theories regarding the origin of the title '''{{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}''' in Japan. It has sometimes been conflated with the [[Big Dipper]] due to the movement of the celestial pole.
[[Emperor Gaozong of Tang]] was named such and lived from 21 July 628<ref name="birth">According to Tang Taizong's biography in the ''[[Old Book of Tang]]'', Li Zhi was born on the ''gengyin'' day in the 6th month of the 2nd year of the ''Zhen'guan'' era of Taizong's reign. This corresponds to 21 Jul 628 in the Gregorian calendar. [(貞觀二年)六月庚寅，皇子治生] ''Old Book of Tang, vol. 2''</ref> – 27 December 683<ref name="death">Volume 203 of ''[[Zizhi Tongjian]]'' recorded that Gaozong died in the night of the ''dingsi'' day of the 12th month of the 1st year of the Hongdao era of his reign. This date corresponds to 27 Dec 683 in the Gregorian calendar. [(弘道元年)十二月，丁巳，...。是夜，....，上崩於貞觀殿。] ''Zizhi Tongjian'', vol.203.</ref>. The term Tenno (天皇) was first attested in 720 with the [[Nihon Shoki]] so this god or the title is attributed as a possible source of the title.
== Overview == Ancient China understood that stars appeared to rotate around a fixed point in the sky, which was called the '''Northern Celestial Pole''' (corresponding to the celestial north pole) and regarded as the '''center of the universe'''. This belief led to its deification and its incorporation into the titles used in {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}} and later in Japan’s imperial system.
During the Tang dynasty, {{ill|Emperor Gaozong of Tang|ja|高宗 (唐)}} took the title of "Heavenly Sovereign" (天皇), and after his death, his consort {{ill|Wu Zetian|lt=Empress Wu Zetian|ja|則天武后}} granted him the posthumous title "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor" (天皇大帝)<ref>Gaozong’s use of the title "Heavenly Sovereign" was a "one-generation personal title" rather than a strict monarchic title. This occurred about a year and a half after {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}} had begun using the title in Japan. See {{ill|Mitsuo Toyama|ja|遠山美都男}}, ''The Origins of the Emperor and Japan: Unraveling the Mystery of the "Great Kings of Asuka,"'' Kodansha Gendai Shinsho, 2003, pp. 271-272.</ref>. This coincided with the early use of the "Emperor" (天皇) title in Japan under {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}}, though scholars debate which came first.
Taoism venerates the supreme deity as {{ill|Jade Emperor|ja|玉皇大帝}}, associating him with the [[:en:Ziwei_enclosure|purple forbidden enclosure]] and the {{ill|North Star|ja|北極星}}<ref group="note">Due to Earth’s precession, the celestial north pole shifted around 500 CE, transitioning from {{ill|Beta Ursae Minoris|lt=Kochab|ja|こぐま座ベータ星}} to {{ill|Polaris|ja|ポラリス (恒星)}}.</ref>. The deity was identified as '''{{ill|Ziwei Emperor|lt=Beiji Ziwei Dadi|ja|北極紫微大帝}}'''. This belief later syncretized with the {{ill|Big Dipper Faith|ja|北斗信仰}}, deifying the Big Dipper as '''{{ill|Beidou Zhenjun|ja|北斗星君}}'''. In time, it further merged with the Buddhist concept of {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}, forming the '''Myōken faith'''. Consequently, "Beiji Ziwei Dadi" (North Pole Purple Tenuous Emperor) and "Beidou Zhenjun" (Big Dipper True Lord) are sometimes considered distinct and sometimes identified as the same deity.
The belief in the North Star's divine power extended to Chinese emperors and Japan’s imperial family. The robes of Chinese emperors featured the {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星}} on the left sleeve and the {{ill|Zhinü|lt=Weaving Maiden|ja|織女}}<ref group="note">This does not refer to {{ill|Vega|ja|ベガ}} alone but to the constellation {{ill|Weaving Maiden Three Stars|ja|織女三星}}, which includes Vega.</ref> on the right. Meanwhile, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} preserves an imperial robe of {{ill|Emperor Komei|ja|孝明天皇}}, with the Big Dipper embroidered at the center of the upper back, though without the Weaving Maiden.
== The Heavenly Sovereign Emperor as a Constellation == [[File:和漢三才図絵「北極紫微垣之図」部分・上下逆.JPG|thumb|230px|right|''{{ill|Wakan Sansai Zue|ja|和漢三才図会}}'': "Map of the Purple Forbidden Enclosure" with ''Beiji Ziwei Dadi'' (北極紫微大帝) and ''Heavenly Sovereign Emperor'' (天皇大帝)]]
Beyond its religious significance, "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor" also referred to an actual asterism. It is mentioned in ''{{ill|Kaiyuan Zhanjing|ja|開元占経}}'', Volume 69, "Ganshi's Central Observations" (甘氏中官占)<ref>Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 48.</ref>. In the ''{{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}'', ''Tianwen Zhi'' (Astronomical Records) states: "Within the 'mouth' is one star called 'Heavenly Sovereign Emperor'" (口中一星を天皇大帝と曰ふ).
This "mouth" refers to the asterism ''Gouchen'' (勾陳), which guarded the celestial palace of the Jade Emperor, the {{ill|Purple Forbidden Enclosure|ja|紫微垣}}<ref>Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 153.</ref>. The star known as "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor" is believed to be {{ill|HD212710|ja|HD212710}}, a 5th magnitude star in {{ill|Cepheus|ja|ケフェウス座}}<ref group="note">Modern identifications were made using the star catalog data from ''Imperial Astronomical Treatise'' (欽定儀象考成) by Tsuchihashi, Chevalier, and Iida. See Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 297.</ref>.
== Worship and Rituals == The {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星}} was seen as a celestial ladle pouring cosmic blessings. The ''{{ill|Records of the Grand Historian|ja|史記}}'' describes the Big Dipper as revolving around the North Star, marking time, seasons, and ensuring the stability of the state<ref>''Shiji'' "Treatise on Astronomy" and ''Star Canon'' (星経), "The Big Dipper" section.</ref>.
The Heavenly Sovereign Emperor is associated with divine regalia, specifically a '''mirror''' and a '''sword'''. These items serve as both ritual implements and symbols of authority.
== Reading and Pronunciation == According to {{ill|Tetsuji Morohashi|ja|諸橋轍次}}’s ''{{ill|Dai Kan-Wa Jiten|ja|大漢和辞典}}'' (1956), the historical reading of "天皇大帝" was ''Ten'wō Taitei'' (てんわうだいてい), pronounced as ''Tennō Daitei'' due to {{ill|Rendaku|ja|連声}}. As a constellation name, it was commonly read as ''Tenkō Taitei'' (てんこうたいてい)<ref>Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 173.</ref>.== See Also == * {{ill|North Star|ja|北極星}} * {{ill|Northern Celestial Pole|ja|北辰}} * {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}} * [[:en:Ziwei_enclosure|purple forbidden enclosure]]

See {{ill|Mitsuo Toyama|ja|遠山美都男}}, ''The Origins of the Emperor and Japan: Unraveling the Mystery of the "Great Kings of Asuka,"'' Kodansha Gendai Shinsho, 2003, pp. 271-272.</ref>. This coincided with the early use of the "Emperor" (天皇) title in Japan under {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}}, though scholars debate which came first.
Taoism venerates the supreme deity as {{ill|Jade Emperor|ja|玉皇大帝}}, associating him with the [[:en:Ziwei_enclosure|purple forbidden enclosure]] and the {{ill|North Star|ja|北極星}}<ref group="note">Due to Earth’s precession, the celestial north pole shifted around 500 CE, transitioning from {{ill|Beta Ursae Minoris|lt=Kochab|ja|こぐま座ベータ星}} to {{ill|Polaris|ja|ポラリス (恒星)}}.</ref>. The deity was identified as '''{{ill|Ziwei Emperor|lt=Beiji Ziwei Dadi|ja|北極紫微大帝}}'''. This belief later syncretized with the {{ill|Big Dipper Faith|ja|北斗信仰}}, deifying the Big Dipper as '''{{ill|Beidou Zhenjun|ja|北斗星君}}'''. In time, it further merged with the Buddhist concept of {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}, forming the '''Myōken faith'''. Consequently, "Beiji Ziwei Dadi" (North Pole Purple Tenuous Emperor) and "Beidou Zhenjun" (Big Dipper True Lord) are sometimes considered distinct and sometimes identified as the same deity.
The belief in the North Star's divine power extended to Chinese emperors and Japan’s imperial family. The robes of Chinese emperors featured the {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星}} on the left sleeve and the {{ill|Zhinü|lt=Weaving Maiden|ja|織女}}<ref group="note">This does not refer to {{ill|Vega|ja|ベガ}} alone but to the constellation {{ill|Weaving Maiden Three Stars|ja|織女三星}}, which includes Vega.</ref> on the right. Meanwhile, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} preserves an imperial robe of {{ill|Emperor Komei|ja|孝明天皇}}, with the Big Dipper embroidered at the center of the upper back, though without the Weaving Maiden.
== The Heavenly Sovereign Emperor as a Constellation == [[File:和漢三才図絵「北極紫微垣之図」部分・上下逆.JPG|thumb|230px|right|''{{ill|Wakan Sansai Zue|ja|和漢三才図会}}'': "Map of the Purple Forbidden Enclosure" with ''Beiji Ziwei Dadi'' (北極紫微大帝) and ''Heavenly Sovereign Emperor'' (天皇大帝)]]
Beyond its religious significance, "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor" also referred to an actual asterism. It is mentioned in ''{{ill|Kaiyuan Zhanjing|ja|開元占経}}'', Volume 69, "Ganshi's Central Observations" (甘氏中官占)<ref>Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 48.</ref>. In the ''{{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}'', ''Tianwen Zhi'' (Astronomical Records) states: "Within the 'mouth' is one star called 'Heavenly Sovereign Emperor'" (口中一星を天皇大帝と曰ふ).
This "mouth" refers to the asterism ''Gouchen'' (勾陳), which guarded the celestial palace of the Jade Emperor, the {{ill|Purple Forbidden Enclosure|ja|紫微垣}}<ref>Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 153.</ref>. The star known as "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor" is believed to be {{ill|HD212710|ja|HD212710}}, a 5th magnitude star in {{ill|Cepheus|ja|ケフェウス座}}<ref group="note">Modern identifications were made using the star catalog data from ''Imperial Astronomical Treatise'' (欽定儀象考成) by Tsuchihashi, Chevalier, and Iida. See Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 297.</ref>.
== Worship and Rituals == The {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星}} was seen as a celestial ladle pouring cosmic blessings. The ''{{ill|Records of the Grand Historian|ja|史記}}'' describes the Big Dipper as revolving around the North Star, marking time, seasons, and ensuring the stability of the state<ref>''Shiji'' "Treatise on Astronomy" and ''Star Canon'' (星経), "The Big Dipper" section.</ref>.
The Heavenly Sovereign Emperor is associated with divine regalia, specifically a '''mirror''' and a '''sword'''. These items serve as both ritual implements and symbols of authority.
== Reading and Pronunciation == According to {{ill|Tetsuji Morohashi|ja|諸橋轍次}}’s ''{{ill|Dai Kan-Wa Jiten|ja|大漢和辞典}}'' (1956), the historical reading of "天皇大帝" was ''Ten'wō Taitei'' (てんわうだいてい), pronounced as ''Tennō Daitei'' due to {{ill|Rendaku|ja|連声}}. As a constellation name, it was commonly read as ''Tenkō Taitei'' (てんこうたいてい)<ref>Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 173.</ref>. == See Also == * {{ill|North Star|ja|北極星}} * {{ill|Northern Celestial Pole|ja|北辰}} * {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}} * [[:en:Ziwei_enclosure|purple forbidden enclosure]]
[[Category:Taoism]] [[Category:Chinese constellations]] [[Category:Four Sovereigns]] [[ja:天皇大帝]]
[[Category:Completion tier 1]][[Category: tier 1]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tianhuang Emperor]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tianhuang Emperor]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Taoism]] [[Category:Chinese constellations]] [[Category:Four heavenly ministers]] [[Category:Expanded pages from Wikipedia]][[en:Tianhuang Emperor]] [[ja:天皇大帝]] ==Notes== <references group="note"/>

{{for|The figure given the posthumous title of "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor"|{{ill|Liu Yan (Southern Han)|ja|劉龑}}}} [[File:天皇大帝.JPG|thumb|Statue of the Heavenly Sovereign Emperor (Tianhuang Dadi) at Okayamajutenkyu, Taiwan]] [[File:Sterneamwalberla2.jpg|thumb|230px|right|Stars rotating around the North Star]] [[File:Draco constellation map.png|thumb|230px|right|Ursa Minor and Draco. The current North Star is marked as "Polaris"]]
'''Heavenly Sovereign Emperor''' (天皇大帝, ''Ten'nō Taitei''<ref>{{Cite book|author=Saburōsuke Tanabe|year=1991|title=Comprehensive Encyclopedia of Japanese Buddhist Statues, Volume 5: Syncretic Deities and Eminent Monks|page=18|publisher={{ill|Gyosei|ja|ぎょうせい}}|isbn=4-324-02117-1}}</ref>) refers to the deification of the '''Northern Celestial Pole''' (北辰, ''Hokushin'')<ref>Hisao Fukushima, ''The Night Sky as Seen by Confucius,'' {{ill|Taishukan Shoten|ja|大修館書店}}, 1997.</ref>. It is considered one of the most prominent theories regarding the origin of the title '''{{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇}}''' in Japan. It has sometimes been conflated with the [[Big Dipper]] due to the movement of the celestial pole.
[[Emperor Gaozong of Tang]] was named such and lived from 21 July 628<ref name="birth">According to Tang Taizong's biography in the ''[[Old Book of Tang]]'', Li Zhi was born on the ''gengyin'' day in the 6th month of the 2nd year of the ''Zhen'guan'' era of Taizong's reign. This corresponds to 21 Jul 628 in the Gregorian calendar. [(貞觀二年)六月庚寅，皇子治生] ''Old Book of Tang, vol. 2''</ref> – 27 December 683<ref name="death">Volume 203 of ''[[Zizhi Tongjian]]'' recorded that Gaozong died in the night of the ''dingsi'' day of the 12th month of the 1st year of the Hongdao era of his reign. This date corresponds to 27 Dec 683 in the Gregorian calendar. [(弘道元年)十二月，丁巳，...。是夜，....，上崩於貞觀殿。] ''Zizhi Tongjian'', vol.203.</ref>. The term Tenno (天皇) was first attested in 720 with the [[Nihon Shoki]] so this god or the title is attributed as a possible source of the title.
== Overview == Ancient China understood that stars appeared to rotate around a fixed point in the sky, which was called the '''Northern Celestial Pole''' (corresponding to the celestial north pole) and regarded as the '''center of the universe'''. This belief led to its deification and its incorporation into the titles used in {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}} and later in Japan’s imperial system.
During the Tang dynasty, {{ill|Emperor Gaozong of Tang|ja|高宗 (唐)}} took the title of "Heavenly Sovereign" (天皇), and after his death, his consort {{ill|Wu Zetian|lt=Empress Wu Zetian|ja|則天武后}} granted him the posthumous title "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor" (天皇大帝)<ref>Gaozong’s use of the title "Heavenly Sovereign" was a "one-generation personal title" rather than a strict monarchic title. This occurred about a year and a half after {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}} had begun using the title in Japan. See {{ill|Mitsuo Toyama|ja|遠山美都男}}, ''The Origins of the Emperor and Japan: Unraveling the Mystery of the "Great Kings of Asuka,"'' Kodansha Gendai Shinsho, 2003, pp. 271-272.</ref>. This coincided with the early use of the "Emperor" (天皇) title in Japan under {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}}, though scholars debate which came first.
Taoism venerates the supreme deity as {{ill|Jade Emperor|ja|玉皇大帝}}, associating him with the [[:en:Ziwei_enclosure|purple forbidden enclosure]] and the {{ill|North Star|ja|北極星}}<ref group="note">Due to Earth’s precession, the celestial north pole shifted around 500 CE, transitioning from {{ill|Beta Ursae Minoris|lt=Kochab|ja|こぐま座ベータ星}} to {{ill|Polaris|ja|ポラリス (恒星)}}.</ref>. The deity was identified as '''{{ill|Ziwei Emperor|lt=Beiji Ziwei Dadi|ja|北極紫微大帝}}'''. This belief later syncretized with the {{ill|Big Dipper Faith|ja|北斗信仰}}, deifying the Big Dipper as '''{{ill|Beidou Zhenjun|ja|北斗星君}}'''. In time, it further merged with the Buddhist concept of {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}, forming the '''Myōken faith'''. Consequently, "Beiji Ziwei Dadi" (North Pole Purple Tenuous Emperor) and "Beidou Zhenjun" (Big Dipper True Lord) are sometimes considered distinct and sometimes identified as the same deity.
The belief in the North Star's divine power extended to Chinese emperors and Japan’s imperial family. The robes of Chinese emperors featured the {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星}} on the left sleeve and the {{ill|Zhinü|lt=Weaving Maiden|ja|織女}}<ref group="note">This does not refer to {{ill|Vega|ja|ベガ}} alone but to the constellation {{ill|Weaving Maiden Three Stars|ja|織女三星}}, which includes Vega.</ref> on the right. Meanwhile, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} preserves an imperial robe of {{ill|Emperor Komei|ja|孝明天皇}}, with the Big Dipper embroidered at the center of the upper back, though without the Weaving Maiden.
== The Heavenly Sovereign Emperor as a Constellation == [[File:和漢三才図絵「北極紫微垣之図」部分・上下逆.JPG|thumb|230px|right|''{{ill|Wakan Sansai Zue|ja|和漢三才図会}}'': "Map of the Purple Forbidden Enclosure" with ''Beiji Ziwei Dadi'' (北極紫微大帝) and ''Heavenly Sovereign Emperor'' (天皇大帝)]]

In the ''{{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}'', ''Tianwen Zhi'' (Astronomical Records) states: "Within the 'mouth' is one star called 'Heavenly Sovereign Emperor'" (口中一星を天皇大帝と曰ふ).
This "mouth" refers to the asterism ''Gouchen'' (勾陳), which guarded the celestial palace of the Jade Emperor, the {{ill|Purple Forbidden Enclosure|ja|紫微垣}}<ref>Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 153.</ref>. The star known as "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor" is believed to be {{ill|HD212710|ja|HD212710}}, a 5th magnitude star in {{ill|Cepheus|ja|ケフェウス座}}<ref group="note">Modern identifications were made using the star catalog data from ''Imperial Astronomical Treatise'' (欽定儀象考成) by Tsuchihashi, Chevalier, and Iida. See Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 297.</ref>.

The ''{{ill|Records of the Grand Historian|ja|史記}}'' describes the Big Dipper as revolving around the North Star, marking time, seasons, and ensuring the stability of the state<ref>''Shiji'' "Treatise on Astronomy" and ''Star Canon'' (星経), "The Big Dipper" section.</ref>.

== Overview == Ancient China understood that stars appeared to rotate around a fixed point in the sky, which was called the '''Northern Celestial Pole''' (corresponding to the celestial north pole) and regarded as the '''center of the universe'''. This belief led to its deification and its incorporation into the titles used in {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教}} and later in Japan’s imperial system.
During the Tang dynasty, {{ill|Emperor Gaozong of Tang|ja|高宗 (唐)}} took the title of "Heavenly Sovereign" (天皇), and after his death, his consort {{ill|Wu Zetian|lt=Empress Wu Zetian|ja|則天武后}} granted him the posthumous title "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor" (天皇大帝)<ref>Gaozong’s use of the title "Heavenly Sovereign" was a "one-generation personal title" rather than a strict monarchic title. This occurred about a year and a half after {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}} had begun using the title in Japan. See {{ill|Mitsuo Toyama|ja|遠山美都男}}, ''The Origins of the Emperor and Japan: Unraveling the Mystery of the "Great Kings of Asuka,"'' Kodansha Gendai Shinsho, 2003, pp. 271-272.</ref>. This coincided with the early use of the "Emperor" (天皇) title in Japan under {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}}, though scholars debate which came first.
Taoism venerates the supreme deity as {{ill|Jade Emperor|ja|玉皇大帝}}, associating him with the [[:en:Ziwei_enclosure|purple forbidden enclosure]] and the {{ill|North Star|ja|北極星}}<ref group="note">Due to Earth’s precession, the celestial north pole shifted around 500 CE, transitioning from {{ill|Beta Ursae Minoris|lt=Kochab|ja|こぐま座ベータ星}} to {{ill|Polaris|ja|ポラリス (恒星)}}.</ref>. The deity was identified as '''{{ill|Ziwei Emperor|lt=Beiji Ziwei Dadi|ja|北極紫微大帝}}'''. This belief later syncretized with the {{ill|Big Dipper Faith|ja|北斗信仰}}, deifying the Big Dipper as '''{{ill|Beidou Zhenjun|ja|北斗星君}}'''. In time, it further merged with the Buddhist concept of {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}, forming the '''Myōken faith'''. Consequently, "Beiji Ziwei Dadi" (North Pole Purple Tenuous Emperor) and "Beidou Zhenjun" (Big Dipper True Lord) are sometimes considered distinct and sometimes identified as the same deity.
The belief in the North Star's divine power extended to Chinese emperors and Japan’s imperial family. The robes of Chinese emperors featured the {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星}} on the left sleeve and the {{ill|Zhinü|lt=Weaving Maiden|ja|織女}}<ref group="note">This does not refer to {{ill|Vega|ja|ベガ}} alone but to the constellation {{ill|Weaving Maiden Three Stars|ja|織女三星}}, which includes Vega.</ref> on the right. Meanwhile, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} preserves an imperial robe of {{ill|Emperor Komei|ja|孝明天皇}}, with the Big Dipper embroidered at the center of the upper back, though without the Weaving Maiden.
== The Heavenly Sovereign Emperor as a Constellation == [[File:和漢三才図絵「北極紫微垣之図」部分・上下逆.JPG|thumb|230px|right|''{{ill|Wakan Sansai Zue|ja|和漢三才図会}}'': "Map of the Purple Forbidden Enclosure" with ''Beiji Ziwei Dadi'' (北極紫微大帝) and ''Heavenly Sovereign Emperor'' (天皇大帝)]]
Beyond its religious significance, "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor" also referred to an actual asterism. It is mentioned in ''{{ill|Kaiyuan Zhanjing|ja|開元占経}}'', Volume 69, "Ganshi's Central Observations" (甘氏中官占)<ref>Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 48.</ref>. In the ''{{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書}}'', ''Tianwen Zhi'' (Astronomical Records) states: "Within the 'mouth' is one star called 'Heavenly Sovereign Emperor'" (口中一星を天皇大帝と曰ふ).
This "mouth" refers to the asterism ''Gouchen'' (勾陳), which guarded the celestial palace of the Jade Emperor, the {{ill|Purple Forbidden Enclosure|ja|紫微垣}}<ref>Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 153.</ref>. The star known as "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor" is believed to be {{ill|HD212710|ja|HD212710}}, a 5th magnitude star in {{ill|Cepheus|ja|ケフェウス座}}<ref group="note">Modern identifications were made using the star catalog data from ''Imperial Astronomical Treatise'' (欽定儀象考成) by Tsuchihashi, Chevalier, and Iida. See Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 297.</ref>.
== Worship and Rituals == The {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星}} was seen as a celestial ladle pouring cosmic blessings. The ''{{ill|Records of the Grand Historian|ja|史記}}'' describes the Big Dipper as revolving around the North Star, marking time, seasons, and ensuring the stability of the state<ref>''Shiji'' "Treatise on Astronomy" and ''Star Canon'' (星経), "The Big Dipper" section.</ref>.
The Heavenly Sovereign Emperor is associated with divine regalia, specifically a '''mirror''' and a '''sword'''. These items serve as both ritual implements and symbols of authority.
== Reading and Pronunciation == According to {{ill|Tetsuji Morohashi|ja|諸橋轍次}}’s ''{{ill|Dai Kan-Wa Jiten|ja|大漢和辞典}}'' (1956), the historical reading of "天皇大帝" was ''Ten'wō Taitei'' (てんわうだいてい), pronounced as ''Tennō Daitei'' due to {{ill|Rendaku|ja|連声}}. As a constellation name, it was commonly read as ''Tenkō Taitei'' (てんこうたいてい)<ref>Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 173.</ref>. == See Also == * {{ill|North Star|ja|北極星}} * {{ill|Northern Celestial Pole|ja|北辰}} * {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}} * [[:en:Ziwei_enclosure|purple forbidden enclosure]]
==Notes== <references group="note"/>
==References==

revision.text:
{{for|The figure given the posthumous title of "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor"|{{ill|Liu Yan (Southern Han)|ja|劉龑|12=draft}}}} [[File:天皇大帝.JPG|thumb|Statue of the Heavenly Sovereign Emperor (Tianhuang Dadi) at Okayamajutenkyu, Taiwan]] [[File:Sterneamwalberla2.jpg|thumb|230px|right|Stars rotating around the North Star]] [[File:Draco constellation map.png|thumb|230px|right|Ursa Minor and Draco. The current North Star is marked as "Polaris"]]
'''Heavenly Sovereign Emperor''' (天皇大帝, ''Ten'nō Taitei''<ref>{{Cite book|author=Saburōsuke Tanabe|year=1991|title=Comprehensive Encyclopedia of Japanese Buddhist Statues, Volume 5: Syncretic Deities and Eminent Monks|page=18|publisher={{ill|Gyosei|ja|ぎょうせい|12=draft}}|isbn=4-324-02117-1}}</ref>) refers to the deification of the '''Northern Celestial Pole''' (北辰, ''Hokushin'')<ref>Hisao Fukushima, ''The Night Sky as Seen by Confucius,'' {{ill|Taishukan Shoten|ja|大修館書店|12=draft}}, 1997.</ref>. It is considered one of the most prominent theories regarding the origin of the title '''{{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇|12=draft}}''' in Japan. It has sometimes been conflated with the [[Big Dipper]] due to the movement of the celestial pole.
[[Emperor Gaozong of Tang]] was named such and lived from 21 July 628<ref name="birth">According to Tang Taizong's biography in the ''[[Old Book of Tang]]'', Li Zhi was born on the ''gengyin'' day in the 6th month of the 2nd year of the ''Zhen'guan'' era of Taizong's reign. This corresponds to 21 Jul 628 in the Gregorian calendar. [(貞觀二年)六月庚寅，皇子治生] ''Old Book of Tang, vol. 2''</ref> – 27 December 683<ref name="death">Volume 203 of ''[[Zizhi Tongjian]]'' recorded that Gaozong died in the night of the ''dingsi'' day of the 12th month of the 1st year of the Hongdao era of his reign. This date corresponds to 27 Dec 683 in the Gregorian calendar. [(弘道元年)十二月，丁巳，...。是夜，....，上崩於貞觀殿。] ''Zizhi Tongjian'', vol.203.</ref>. The term Tenno (天皇) was first attested in 720 with the [[Nihon Shoki]] so this god or the title is attributed as a possible source of the title.
== Overview == Ancient China understood that stars appeared to rotate around a fixed point in the sky, which was called the '''Northern Celestial Pole''' (corresponding to the celestial north pole) and regarded as the '''center of the universe'''. This belief led to its deification and its incorporation into the titles used in {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教|12=draft}} and later in Japan’s imperial system.
During the Tang dynasty, {{ill|Emperor Gaozong of Tang|ja|高宗 (唐)|12=draft}} took the title of "Heavenly Sovereign" (天皇), and after his death, his consort {{ill|Wu Zetian|lt=Empress Wu Zetian|ja|則天武后|12=draft}} granted him the posthumous title "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor" (天皇大帝)<ref>Gaozong’s use of the title "Heavenly Sovereign" was a "one-generation personal title" rather than a strict monarchic title. This occurred about a year and a half after {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=draft}} had begun using the title in Japan. See {{ill|Mitsuo Toyama|ja|遠山美都男|12=draft}}, ''The Origins of the Emperor and Japan: Unraveling the Mystery of the "Great Kings of Asuka,"'' Kodansha Gendai Shinsho, 2003, pp. 271-272.</ref>. Taoism venerates the supreme deity as {{ill|Jade Emperor|ja|玉皇大帝|12=draft}}, associating him with the [[:en:Ziwei_enclosure|purple forbidden enclosure]] and the {{ill|North Star|ja|北極星|12=draft}}<ref group="note">Due to Earth’s precession, the celestial north pole shifted around 500 CE, transitioning from {{ill|Beta Ursae Minoris|lt=Kochab|ja|こぐま座ベータ星|12=draft}} to {{ill|Polaris|ja|ポラリス (恒星)|12=draft}}.</ref>. The deity was identified as '''{{ill|Ziwei Emperor|lt=Beiji Ziwei Dadi|ja|北極紫微大帝|12=draft}}'''. This belief later syncretized with the {{ill|Big Dipper Faith|ja|北斗信仰|12=draft}}, deifying the Big Dipper as '''{{ill|Beidou Zhenjun|ja|北斗星君|12=draft}}'''. The belief in the North Star's divine power extended to Chinese emperors and Japan’s imperial family. The robes of Chinese emperors featured the {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星|12=draft}} on the left sleeve and the {{ill|Zhinü|lt=Weaving Maiden|ja|織女|12=draft}}<ref group="note">This does not refer to {{ill|Vega|ja|ベガ|12=draft}} alone but to the constellation {{ill|Weaving Maiden Three Stars|ja|織女三星|12=draft}}, which includes Vega.</ref> on the right. Meanwhile, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁|12=draft}} preserves an imperial robe of {{ill|Emperor Komei|ja|孝明天皇|12=draft}}, with the Big Dipper embroidered at the center of the upper back, though without the Weaving Maiden.

[[Emperor Gaozong of Tang]] was named such and lived from 21 July 628<ref name="birth">According to Tang Taizong's biography in the ''[[Old Book of Tang]]'', Li Zhi was born on the ''gengyin'' day in the 6th month of the 2nd year of the ''Zhen'guan'' era of Taizong's reign. This corresponds to 21 Jul 628 in the Gregorian calendar. [(貞觀二年)六月庚寅，皇子治生] ''Old Book of Tang, vol. 2''</ref> – 27 December 683<ref name="death">Volume 203 of ''[[Zizhi Tongjian]]'' recorded that Gaozong died in the night of the ''dingsi'' day of the 12th month of the 1st year of the Hongdao era of his reign. This date corresponds to 27 Dec 683 in the Gregorian calendar. [(弘道元年)十二月，丁巳，...。是夜，....，上崩於貞觀殿。] ''Zizhi Tongjian'', vol.203.</ref>. The term Tenno (天皇) was first attested in 720 with the [[Nihon Shoki]] so this god or the title is attributed as a possible source of the title.
== Overview == Ancient China understood that stars appeared to rotate around a fixed point in the sky, which was called the '''Northern Celestial Pole''' (corresponding to the celestial north pole) and regarded as the '''center of the universe'''. This belief led to its deification and its incorporation into the titles used in {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教|12=draft}} and later in Japan’s imperial system.
During the Tang dynasty, {{ill|Emperor Gaozong of Tang|ja|高宗 (唐)|12=draft}} took the title of "Heavenly Sovereign" (天皇), and after his death, his consort {{ill|Wu Zetian|lt=Empress Wu Zetian|ja|則天武后|12=draft}} granted him the posthumous title "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor" (天皇大帝)<ref>Gaozong’s use of the title "Heavenly Sovereign" was a "one-generation personal title" rather than a strict monarchic title. This occurred about a year and a half after {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=draft}} had begun using the title in Japan. See {{ill|Mitsuo Toyama|ja|遠山美都男|12=draft}}, ''The Origins of the Emperor and Japan: Unraveling the Mystery of the "Great Kings of Asuka,"'' Kodansha Gendai Shinsho, 2003, pp. 271-272.</ref>. This coincided with the early use of the "Emperor" (天皇) title in Japan under {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=draft}}, though scholars debate which came first.
Taoism venerates the supreme deity as {{ill|Jade Emperor|ja|玉皇大帝|12=draft}}, associating him with the [[:en:Ziwei_enclosure|purple forbidden enclosure]] and the {{ill|North Star|ja|北極星|12=draft}}<ref group="note">Due to Earth’s precession, the celestial north pole shifted around 500 CE, transitioning from {{ill|Beta Ursae Minoris|lt=Kochab|ja|こぐま座ベータ星|12=draft}} to {{ill|Polaris|ja|ポラリス (恒星)|12=draft}}.</ref>. The deity was identified as '''{{ill|Ziwei Emperor|lt=Beiji Ziwei Dadi|ja|北極紫微大帝|12=draft}}'''. This belief later syncretized with the {{ill|Big Dipper Faith|ja|北斗信仰|12=draft}}, deifying the Big Dipper as '''{{ill|Beidou Zhenjun|ja|北斗星君|12=draft}}'''. In time, it further merged with the Buddhist concept of {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩|12=draft}}, forming the '''Myōken faith'''. Consequently, "Beiji Ziwei Dadi" (North Pole Purple Tenuous Emperor) and "Beidou Zhenjun" (Big Dipper True Lord) are sometimes considered distinct and sometimes identified as the same deity.
The belief in the North Star's divine power extended to Chinese emperors and Japan’s imperial family. The robes of Chinese emperors featured the {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星|12=draft}} on the left sleeve and the {{ill|Zhinü|lt=Weaving Maiden|ja|織女|12=draft}}<ref group="note">This does not refer to {{ill|Vega|ja|ベガ|12=draft}} alone but to the constellation {{ill|Weaving Maiden Three Stars|ja|織女三星|12=draft}}, which includes Vega.</ref> on the right. Meanwhile, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁|12=draft}} preserves an imperial robe of {{ill|Emperor Komei|ja|孝明天皇|12=draft}}, with the Big Dipper embroidered at the center of the upper back, though without the Weaving Maiden.
== The Heavenly Sovereign Emperor as a Constellation == [[File:和漢三才図絵「北極紫微垣之図」部分・上下逆.JPG|thumb|230px|right|''{{ill|Wakan Sansai Zue|ja|和漢三才図会|12=draft}}'': "Map of the Purple Forbidden Enclosure" with ''Beiji Ziwei Dadi'' (北極紫微大帝) and ''Heavenly Sovereign Emperor'' (天皇大帝)]]
Beyond its religious significance, "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor" also referred to an actual asterism. It is mentioned in ''{{ill|Kaiyuan Zhanjing|ja|開元占経|12=draft}}'', Volume 69, "Ganshi's Central Observations" (甘氏中官占)<ref>Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 48.</ref>. In the ''{{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書|12=draft}}'', ''Tianwen Zhi'' (Astronomical Records) states: "Within the 'mouth' is one star called 'Heavenly Sovereign Emperor'" (口中一星を天皇大帝と曰ふ).
This "mouth" refers to the asterism ''Gouchen'' (勾陳), which guarded the celestial palace of the Jade Emperor, the {{ill|Purple Forbidden Enclosure|ja|紫微垣|12=draft}}<ref>Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 153.</ref>. The star known as "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor" is believed to be {{ill|HD212710|ja|HD212710|12=draft}}, a 5th magnitude star in {{ill|Cepheus|ja|ケフェウス座|12=draft}}<ref group="note">Modern identifications were made using the star catalog data from ''Imperial Astronomical Treatise'' (欽定儀象考成) by Tsuchihashi, Chevalier, and Iida. See Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 297.</ref>.
== Worship and Rituals == The {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星|12=draft}} was seen as a celestial ladle pouring cosmic blessings. The ''{{ill|Records of the Grand Historian|ja|史記|12=draft}}'' describes the Big Dipper as revolving around the North Star, marking time, seasons, and ensuring the stability of the state<ref>''Shiji'' "Treatise on Astronomy" and ''Star Canon'' (星経), "The Big Dipper" section.</ref>.
The Heavenly Sovereign Emperor is associated with divine regalia, specifically a '''mirror''' and a '''sword'''. These items serve as both ritual implements and symbols of authority.
== Reading and Pronunciation == According to {{ill|Tetsuji Morohashi|ja|諸橋轍次|12=draft}}’s ''{{ill|Dai Kan-Wa Jiten|ja|大漢和辞典|12=draft}}'' (1956), the historical reading of "天皇大帝" was ''Ten'wō Taitei'' (てんわうだいてい), pronounced as ''Tennō Daitei'' due to {{ill|Rendaku|ja|連声|12=draft}}. As a constellation name, it was commonly read as ''Tenkō Taitei'' (てんこうたいてい)<ref>Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 173.</ref>. == See Also == * {{ill|North Star|ja|北極星|12=draft}} * {{ill|Northern Celestial Pole|ja|北辰|12=draft}} * {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩|12=draft}} * [[:en:Ziwei_enclosure|purple forbidden enclosure]]
==Notes== <references group="note"/>
==References==
{{draft categories| [[Category:Taoism]] [[Category:Chinese constellations]] [[Category:Four Sovereigns]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Four heavenly ministers]] [[Category:Expanded pages from Wikipedia]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:return the see also]] }} [[ja:天皇大帝]] [[sn:Tianhuang Emperor]] [[en:Tianhuang Emperor]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Tianhuang Emperor}}

The current North Star is marked as "Polaris"]]

revision.text:
{{for|The figure given the posthumous title of "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor"|{{ill|Liu Yan (Southern Han)|ja|劉龑|12=draft|12=draft}}}} [[File:天皇大帝.JPG|thumb|Statue of the Heavenly Sovereign Emperor (Tianhuang Dadi) at Okayamajutenkyu, Taiwan]] [[File:Sterneamwalberla2.jpg|thumb|230px|right|Stars rotating around the North Star]] [[File:Draco constellation map.png|thumb|230px|right|Ursa Minor and Draco. The current North Star is marked as "Polaris"]]
'''Heavenly Sovereign Emperor''' (天皇大帝, ''Ten'nō Taitei''<ref>{{Cite book|author=Saburōsuke Tanabe|year=1991|title=Comprehensive Encyclopedia of Japanese Buddhist Statues, Volume 5: Syncretic Deities and Eminent Monks|page=18|publisher={{ill|Gyosei|ja|ぎょうせい|12=draft|12=draft}}|isbn=4-324-02117-1}}</ref>) refers to the deification of the '''Northern Celestial Pole''' (北辰, ''Hokushin'')<ref>Hisao Fukushima, ''The Night Sky as Seen by Confucius,'' {{ill|Taishukan Shoten|ja|大修館書店|12=draft|12=draft}}, 1997.</ref>. It is considered one of the most prominent theories regarding the origin of the title '''{{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇|12=draft|12=draft}}''' in Japan. It has sometimes been conflated with the [[Big Dipper]] due to the movement of the celestial pole.
[[Emperor Gaozong of Tang]] was named such and lived from 21 July 628<ref name="birth">According to Tang Taizong's biography in the ''[[Old Book of Tang]]'', Li Zhi was born on the ''gengyin'' day in the 6th month of the 2nd year of the ''Zhen'guan'' era of Taizong's reign. This corresponds to 21 Jul 628 in the Gregorian calendar. [(貞觀二年)六月庚寅，皇子治生] ''Old Book of Tang, vol. 2''</ref> – 27 December 683<ref name="death">Volume 203 of ''[[Zizhi Tongjian]]'' recorded that Gaozong died in the night of the ''dingsi'' day of the 12th month of the 1st year of the Hongdao era of his reign. This date corresponds to 27 Dec 683 in the Gregorian calendar. [(弘道元年)十二月，丁巳，...。是夜，....，上崩於貞觀殿。] ''Zizhi Tongjian'', vol.203.</ref>. The term Tenno (天皇) was first attested in 720 with the [[Nihon Shoki]] so this god or the title is attributed as a possible source of the title.
== Overview == Ancient China understood that stars appeared to rotate around a fixed point in the sky, which was called the '''Northern Celestial Pole''' (corresponding to the celestial north pole) and regarded as the '''center of the universe'''. This belief led to its deification and its incorporation into the titles used in {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教|12=draft|12=draft}} and later in Japan’s imperial system.
During the Tang dynasty, {{ill|Emperor Gaozong of Tang|ja|高宗 (唐)|12=draft|12=draft}} took the title of "Heavenly Sovereign" (天皇), and after his death, his consort {{ill|Wu Zetian|lt=Empress Wu Zetian|ja|則天武后|12=draft|12=draft}} granted him the posthumous title "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor" (天皇大帝)<ref>Gaozong’s use of the title "Heavenly Sovereign" was a "one-generation personal title" rather than a strict monarchic title. This occurred about a year and a half after {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=draft|12=draft}} had begun using the title in Japan. See {{ill|Mitsuo Toyama|ja|遠山美都男|12=draft|12=draft}}, ''The Origins of the Emperor and Japan: Unraveling the Mystery of the "Great Kings of Asuka,"'' Kodansha Gendai Shinsho, 2003, pp. 271-272.</ref>. This coincided with the early use of the "Emperor" (天皇) title in Japan under {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=draft|12=draft}}, though scholars debate which came first.
Taoism venerates the supreme deity as {{ill|Jade Emperor|ja|玉皇大帝|12=draft|12=draft}}, associating him with the [[:en:Ziwei_enclosure|purple forbidden enclosure]] and the {{ill|North Star|ja|北極星|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref group="note">Due to Earth’s precession, the celestial north pole shifted around 500 CE, transitioning from {{ill|Beta Ursae Minoris|lt=Kochab|ja|こぐま座ベータ星|12=draft|12=draft}} to {{ill|Polaris|ja|ポラリス (恒星)|12=draft|12=draft}}.</ref>. The deity was identified as '''{{ill|Ziwei Emperor|lt=Beiji Ziwei Dadi|ja|北極紫微大帝|12=draft|12=draft}}'''. This belief later syncretized with the {{ill|Big Dipper Faith|ja|北斗信仰|12=draft|12=draft}}, deifying the Big Dipper as '''{{ill|Beidou Zhenjun|ja|北斗星君|12=draft|12=draft}}'''. In time, it further merged with the Buddhist concept of {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩|12=draft|12=draft}}, forming the '''Myōken faith'''. Consequently, "Beiji Ziwei Dadi" (North Pole Purple Tenuous Emperor) and "Beidou Zhenjun" (Big Dipper True Lord) are sometimes considered distinct and sometimes identified as the same deity.
The belief in the North Star's divine power extended to Chinese emperors and Japan’s imperial family. The robes of Chinese emperors featured the {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星|12=draft|12=draft}} on the left sleeve and the {{ill|Zhinü|lt=Weaving Maiden|ja|織女|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref group="note">This does not refer to {{ill|Vega|ja|ベガ|12=draft|12=draft}} alone but to the constellation {{ill|Weaving Maiden Three Stars|ja|織女三星|12=draft|12=draft}}, which includes Vega.</ref> on the right. Meanwhile, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁|12=draft|12=draft}} preserves an imperial robe of {{ill|Emperor Komei|ja|孝明天皇|12=draft|12=draft}}, with the Big Dipper embroidered at the center of the upper back, though without the Weaving Maiden.
== The Heavenly Sovereign Emperor as a Constellation == [[File:和漢三才図絵「北極紫微垣之図」部分・上下逆.JPG|thumb|230px|right|''{{ill|Wakan Sansai Zue|ja|和漢三才図会|12=draft|12=draft}}'': "Map of the Purple Forbidden Enclosure" with ''Beiji Ziwei Dadi'' (北極紫微大帝) and ''Heavenly Sovereign Emperor'' (天皇大帝)]]
Beyond its religious significance, "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor" also referred to an actual asterism. It is mentioned in ''{{ill|Kaiyuan Zhanjing|ja|開元占経|12=draft|12=draft}}'', Volume 69, "Ganshi's Central Observations" (甘氏中官占)<ref>Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 48.</ref>. In the ''{{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書|12=draft|12=draft}}'', ''Tianwen Zhi'' (Astronomical Records) states: "Within the 'mouth' is one star called 'Heavenly Sovereign Emperor'" (口中一星を天皇大帝と曰ふ).
This "mouth" refers to the asterism ''Gouchen'' (勾陳), which guarded the celestial palace of the Jade Emperor, the {{ill|Purple Forbidden Enclosure|ja|紫微垣|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref>Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 153.</ref>. The star known as "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor" is believed to be {{ill|HD212710|ja|HD212710|12=draft|12=draft}}, a 5th magnitude star in {{ill|Cepheus|ja|ケフェウス座|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref group="note">Modern identifications were made using the star catalog data from ''Imperial Astronomical Treatise'' (欽定儀象考成) by Tsuchihashi, Chevalier, and Iida. See Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 297.</ref>.
== Worship and Rituals == The {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星|12=draft|12=draft}} was seen as a celestial ladle pouring cosmic blessings. The ''{{ill|Records of the Grand Historian|ja|史記|12=draft|12=draft}}'' describes the Big Dipper as revolving around the North Star, marking time, seasons, and ensuring the stability of the state<ref>''Shiji'' "Treatise on Astronomy" and ''Star Canon'' (星経), "The Big Dipper" section.</ref>.
The Heavenly Sovereign Emperor is associated with divine regalia, specifically a '''mirror''' and a '''sword'''. These items serve as both ritual implements and symbols of authority.
== Reading and Pronunciation == According to {{ill|Tetsuji Morohashi|ja|諸橋轍次|12=draft|12=draft}}’s ''{{ill|Dai Kan-Wa Jiten|ja|大漢和辞典|12=draft|12=draft}}'' (1956), the historical reading of "天皇大帝" was ''Ten'wō Taitei'' (てんわうだいてい), pronounced as ''Tennō Daitei'' due to {{ill|Rendaku|ja|連声|12=draft|12=draft}}. As a constellation name, it was commonly read as ''Tenkō Taitei'' (てんこうたいてい)<ref>Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 173.</ref>. == See Also == * {{ill|North Star|ja|北極星|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Northern Celestial Pole|ja|北辰|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩|12=draft|12=draft}} * [[:en:Ziwei_enclosure|purple forbidden enclosure]]
==Notes== <references group="note"/>
==References==

}}

[[ja:天皇大帝]] [[sn:Tianhuang Emperor]] [[en:Tianhuang Emperor]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:Tianhuang Emperor}}

== The Heavenly Sovereign Emperor as a Constellation == [[File:和漢三才図絵「北極紫微垣之図」部分・上下逆.JPG|thumb|230px|right|''{{ill|Wakan Sansai Zue|ja|和漢三才図会|12=draft|12=draft}}'': "Map of the Purple Forbidden Enclosure" with ''Beiji Ziwei Dadi'' (北極紫微大帝) and ''Heavenly Sovereign Emperor'' (天皇大帝)]]
Beyond its religious significance, "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor" also referred to an actual asterism. It is mentioned in ''{{ill|Kaiyuan Zhanjing|ja|開元占経|12=draft|12=draft}}'', Volume 69, "Ganshi's Central Observations" (甘氏中官占)<ref>Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 48.</ref>. In the ''{{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書|12=draft|12=draft}}'', ''Tianwen Zhi'' (Astronomical Records) states: "Within the 'mouth' is one star called 'Heavenly Sovereign Emperor'" (口中一星を天皇大帝と曰ふ).
This "mouth" refers to the asterism ''Gouchen'' (勾陳), which guarded the celestial palace of the Jade Emperor, the {{ill|Purple Forbidden Enclosure|ja|紫微垣|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref>Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 153.</ref>. The star known as "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor" is believed to be {{ill|HD212710|ja|HD212710|12=draft|12=draft}}, a 5th magnitude star in {{ill|Cepheus|ja|ケフェウス座|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref group="note">Modern identifications were made using the star catalog data from ''Imperial Astronomical Treatise'' (欽定儀象考成) by Tsuchihashi, Chevalier, and Iida. See Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 297.</ref>.
== Worship and Rituals == The {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星|12=draft|12=draft}} was seen as a celestial ladle pouring cosmic blessings. The ''{{ill|Records of the Grand Historian|ja|史記|12=draft|12=draft}}'' describes the Big Dipper as revolving around the North Star, marking time, seasons, and ensuring the stability of the state<ref>''Shiji'' "Treatise on Astronomy" and ''Star Canon'' (星経), "The Big Dipper" section.</ref>.
The Heavenly Sovereign Emperor is associated with divine regalia, specifically a '''mirror''' and a '''sword'''. These items serve as both ritual implements and symbols of authority.
== Reading and Pronunciation == According to {{ill|Tetsuji Morohashi|ja|諸橋轍次|12=draft|12=draft}}’s ''{{ill|Dai Kan-Wa Jiten|ja|大漢和辞典|12=draft|12=draft}}'' (1956), the historical reading of "天皇大帝" was ''Ten'wō Taitei'' (てんわうだいてい), pronounced as ''Tennō Daitei'' due to {{ill|Rendaku|ja|連声|12=draft|12=draft}}. As a constellation name, it was commonly read as ''Tenkō Taitei'' (てんこうたいてい)<ref>Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 173.</ref>. == See Also == * {{ill|North Star|ja|北極星|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Northern Celestial Pole|ja|北辰|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩|12=draft|12=draft}} * [[:en:Ziwei_enclosure|purple forbidden enclosure]]
==Notes== <references group="note"/>
==References==

{{draft categories| [[Category:Taoism]] [[Category:Chinese constellations]] [[Category:Four Sovereigns]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Four heavenly ministers]] [[Category:Expanded pages from Wikipedia]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tianhuang Emperor]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tianhuang Emperor]][[Category:qq]] }}
[[ja:天皇大帝]] [[sn:Tianhuang Emperor]] [[en:Tianhuang Emperor]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:Tianhuang Emperor}}

{{for|The figure given the posthumous title of "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor"|{{ill|Liu Yan (Southern Han)|ja|劉龑|12=draft|12=draft}}}} [[File:天皇大帝.JPG|thumb|Statue of the Heavenly Sovereign Emperor (Tianhuang Dadi) at Okayamajutenkyu, Taiwan]] [[File:Sterneamwalberla2.jpg|thumb|230px|right|Stars rotating around the North Star]] [[File:Draco constellation map.png|thumb|230px|right|Ursa Minor and Draco. The current North Star is marked as "Polaris"]]
'''Heavenly Sovereign Emperor''' (天皇大帝, ''Ten'nō Taitei''<ref>{{Cite book|author=Saburōsuke Tanabe|year=1991|title=Comprehensive Encyclopedia of Japanese Buddhist Statues, Volume 5: Syncretic Deities and Eminent Monks|page=18|publisher={{ill|Gyosei|ja|ぎょうせい|12=draft|12=draft}}|isbn=4-324-02117-1}}</ref>) refers to the deification of the '''Northern Celestial Pole''' (北辰, ''Hokushin'')<ref>Hisao Fukushima, ''The Night Sky as Seen by Confucius,'' {{ill|Taishukan Shoten|ja|大修館書店|12=draft|12=draft}}, 1997.</ref>. It is considered one of the most prominent theories regarding the origin of the title '''{{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇|12=draft|12=draft}}''' in Japan. It has sometimes been conflated with the [[Big Dipper]] due to the movement of the celestial pole.
[[Emperor Gaozong of Tang]] was named such and lived from 21 July 628<ref name="birth">According to Tang Taizong's biography in the ''[[Old Book of Tang]]'', Li Zhi was born on the ''gengyin'' day in the 6th month of the 2nd year of the ''Zhen'guan'' era of Taizong's reign. This corresponds to 21 Jul 628 in the Gregorian calendar. [(貞觀二年)六月庚寅，皇子治生] ''Old Book of Tang, vol. 2''</ref> – 27 December 683<ref name="death">Volume 203 of ''[[Zizhi Tongjian]]'' recorded that Gaozong died in the night of the ''dingsi'' day of the 12th month of the 1st year of the Hongdao era of his reign. This date corresponds to 27 Dec 683 in the Gregorian calendar. [(弘道元年)十二月，丁巳，...。是夜，....，上崩於貞觀殿。] ''Zizhi Tongjian'', vol.203.</ref>. The term Tenno (天皇) was first attested in 720 with the [[Nihon Shoki]] so this god or the title is attributed as a possible source of the title.
== Overview == Ancient China understood that stars appeared to rotate around a fixed point in the sky, which was called the '''Northern Celestial Pole''' (corresponding to the celestial north pole) and regarded as the '''center of the universe'''. This belief led to its deification and its incorporation into the titles used in {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教|12=draft|12=draft}} and later in Japan’s imperial system.
During the Tang dynasty, {{ill|Emperor Gaozong of Tang|ja|高宗 (唐)|12=draft|12=draft}} took the title of "Heavenly Sovereign" (天皇), and after his death, his consort {{ill|Wu Zetian|lt=Empress Wu Zetian|ja|則天武后|12=draft|12=draft}} granted him the posthumous title "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor" (天皇大帝)<ref>Gaozong’s use of the title "Heavenly Sovereign" was a "one-generation personal title" rather than a strict monarchic title. This occurred about a year and a half after {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=draft|12=draft}} had begun using the title in Japan. See {{ill|Mitsuo Toyama|ja|遠山美都男|12=draft|12=draft}}, ''The Origins of the Emperor and Japan: Unraveling the Mystery of the "Great Kings of Asuka,"'' Kodansha Gendai Shinsho, 2003, pp. 271-272.</ref>. This coincided with the early use of the "Emperor" (天皇) title in Japan under {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=draft|12=draft}}, though scholars debate which came first.
Taoism venerates the supreme deity as {{ill|Jade Emperor|ja|玉皇大帝|12=draft|12=draft}}, associating him with the [[:en:Ziwei_enclosure|purple forbidden enclosure]] and the {{ill|North Star|ja|北極星|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref group="note">Due to Earth’s precession, the celestial north pole shifted around 500 CE, transitioning from {{ill|Beta Ursae Minoris|lt=Kochab|ja|こぐま座ベータ星|12=draft|12=draft}} to {{ill|Polaris|ja|ポラリス (恒星)|12=draft|12=draft}}.</ref>. The deity was identified as '''{{ill|Ziwei Emperor|lt=Beiji Ziwei Dadi|ja|北極紫微大帝|12=draft|12=draft}}'''. This belief later syncretized with the {{ill|Big Dipper Faith|ja|北斗信仰|12=draft|12=draft}}, deifying the Big Dipper as '''{{ill|Beidou Zhenjun|ja|北斗星君|12=draft|12=draft}}'''. In time, it further merged with the Buddhist concept of {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩|12=draft|12=draft}}, forming the '''Myōken faith'''. Consequently, "Beiji Ziwei Dadi" (North Pole Purple Tenuous Emperor) and "Beidou Zhenjun" (Big Dipper True Lord) are sometimes considered distinct and sometimes identified as the same deity.
The belief in the North Star's divine power extended to Chinese emperors and Japan’s imperial family. The robes of Chinese emperors featured the {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星|12=draft|12=draft}} on the left sleeve and the {{ill|Zhinü|lt=Weaving Maiden|ja|織女|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref group="note">This does not refer to {{ill|Vega|ja|ベガ|12=draft|12=draft}} alone but to the constellation {{ill|Weaving Maiden Three Stars|ja|織女三星|12=draft|12=draft}}, which includes Vega.</ref> on the right. Meanwhile, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁|12=draft|12=draft}} preserves an imperial robe of {{ill|Emperor Komei|ja|孝明天皇|12=draft|12=draft}}, with the Big Dipper embroidered at the center of the upper back, though without the Weaving Maiden.
== The Heavenly Sovereign Emperor as a Constellation == [[File:和漢三才図絵「北極紫微垣之図」部分・上下逆.JPG|thumb|230px|right|''{{ill|Wakan Sansai Zue|ja|和漢三才図会|12=draft|12=draft}}'': "Map of the Purple Forbidden Enclosure" with ''Beiji Ziwei Dadi'' (北極紫微大帝) and ''Heavenly Sovereign Emperor'' (天皇大帝)]]
Beyond its religious significance, "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor" also referred to an actual asterism. It is mentioned in ''{{ill|Kaiyuan Zhanjing|ja|開元占経|12=draft|12=draft}}'', Volume 69, "Ganshi's Central Observations" (甘氏中官占)<ref>Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 48.</ref>. In the ''{{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書|12=draft|12=draft}}'', ''Tianwen Zhi'' (Astronomical Records) states: "Within the 'mouth' is one star called 'Heavenly Sovereign Emperor'" (口中一星を天皇大帝と曰ふ).
This "mouth" refers to the asterism ''Gouchen'' (勾陳), which guarded the celestial palace of the Jade Emperor, the {{ill|Purple Forbidden Enclosure|ja|紫微垣|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref>Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 153.</ref>. The star known as "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor" is believed to be {{ill|HD212710|ja|HD212710|12=draft|12=draft}}, a 5th magnitude star in {{ill|Cepheus|ja|ケフェウス座|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref group="note">Modern identifications were made using the star catalog data from ''Imperial Astronomical Treatise'' (欽定儀象考成) by Tsuchihashi, Chevalier, and Iida. See Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 297.</ref>.
== Worship and Rituals == The {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星|12=draft|12=draft}} was seen as a celestial ladle pouring cosmic blessings. The ''{{ill|Records of the Grand Historian|ja|史記|12=draft|12=draft}}'' describes the Big Dipper as revolving around the North Star, marking time, seasons, and ensuring the stability of the state<ref>''Shiji'' "Treatise on Astronomy" and ''Star Canon'' (星経), "The Big Dipper" section.</ref>.
The Heavenly Sovereign Emperor is associated with divine regalia, specifically a '''mirror''' and a '''sword'''. These items serve as both ritual implements and symbols of authority.
== Reading and Pronunciation == According to {{ill|Tetsuji Morohashi|ja|諸橋轍次|12=draft|12=draft}}’s ''{{ill|Dai Kan-Wa Jiten|ja|大漢和辞典|12=draft|12=draft}}'' (1956), the historical reading of "天皇大帝" was ''Ten'wō Taitei'' (てんわうだいてい), pronounced as ''Tennō Daitei'' due to {{ill|Rendaku|ja|連声|12=draft|12=draft}}. As a constellation name, it was commonly read as ''Tenkō Taitei'' (てんこうたいてい)<ref>Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 173.</ref>. == See Also == * {{ill|North Star|ja|北極星|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Northern Celestial Pole|ja|北辰|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩|12=draft|12=draft}} * [[:en:Ziwei_enclosure|purple forbidden enclosure]]

revision.text:
{{for|「天皇大帝」の諡号を贈られた人物|劉龑}} [[画像:天皇大帝.JPG|thumb|天皇大帝の像（岡山寿天宮、台湾）]] [[画像:Sterneamwalberla2.jpg|thumb|230px|right|北極星を中心として回転する星々]] [[画像:Draco constellation map.png|thumb|230px|right|こぐま座とりゅう座。現在の北極星は図中の「Polaris」]] '''天皇大帝'''（てんのうたいてい{{Cite book|和書|author=田辺三郎助|year=1991|title=日本の仏像大百科〈5巻〉：習合神・高僧|page=18|publisher=[[ぎょうせい]]|isbn=4-324-02117-1}}、てんのうだいてい）は'''神格化'''された[[北辰]]（[[天の北極]]）福島久雄 『孔子の見た星空』 [[大修館書店]]、1997年。のこと。後述するように、日本における'''[[天皇]]'''という称号の起源の有力な候補の一つと考えられている。[[北斗七星]]と混同されることもある。 == 概要 == 古代中国では地上からは天空のある一点を中心として星々が巡っているように見えることを知っており、そこを'''北辰'''と呼び（天の北極に該当する）、'''宇宙の中心'''と考えられていた。そして神格化され、[[道教]]や日本で使われる称号の[[天皇]]にも取り入れられたとする説がある。 中国の[[唐]]の[[高宗 (唐)|高宗]]は 「天皇」 と称し、死後は皇后の[[則天武后]]によって 「天皇大帝」 の[[諡]]（おくりな）が付けられた高宗の天皇号は、「一代限りの個人的な称号」であって、厳密な意味での君主号ではなく、天武天皇はその1年半前に即位し、天皇号を称している。[[遠山美都男]]『天皇と日本の起源「飛鳥の大王」の謎を解く』（[[講談社現代新書]]、2003年）pp.271-272.。これは日本の[[天武天皇]]による 「天皇」 の号の使用開始とほぼ同時期であるが、どちらが先であるかは研究者間でも結論が出ていない。 道教では最高神を「[[玉皇大帝]]」としており、これを[[月宿|星宿]]における[[紫微垣]]にある北極星西暦500年頃に地球の[[歳差]]のために[[天の北極]]が移動するため、北極星は[[こぐま座ベータ星|こぐま座のβ星]]から[[ポラリス (恒星)|現在の北極星（α星）]]に移っている。に同定して「'''[[北極紫微大帝]]'''」としていた。のちにこれに[[北斗七星]]を神格化した北斗信仰における 「'''[[北斗星君|北斗真君]]'''」とが[[習合]]し、さらに星を仏教における[[妙見菩薩]]に見立てた妙見信仰が生まれた。このため、現在においても「北極紫微大帝（北極大帝・紫微大帝）」 と 「北斗真君」とは、本来は別の神であるとして分ける場合と、同一視する場合とがある。 中国の[[皇帝]]や日本の[[皇室]]の北斗信仰にもこの同一視が見られる。中国の皇帝の祭服の左袖には北斗七星、右袖には[[織女]]これは、いわゆる 「[[ベガ|おりひめ]]」 そのものではなく、「おりひめ」 を含む星座'''織女三星'''のことである。がデザインされている。[[宮内庁]]所蔵の[[孝明天皇]]の礼服 即位の大礼、元旦の朝賀の際のもの。は背中の中央上部にも北斗七星が置かれている。ただし、織女はない。 == 星座（星官）としての天皇大帝 == [[Image:和漢三才図絵「北極紫微垣之図」部分・上下逆.JPG|thumb|230px|right|『[[和漢三才図会]]』 「北極[[紫微垣]]之図」（部分）
'''北極'''（[[きりん座]] HD112028）と'''天皇大帝'''（ケフェウス座 HD212710）に朱点を加えた。両星の間にあるのが現在の北極星（こぐま座α星）]] 現在の北極星（こぐま座α星）は小熊の尻尾の先端にあるが、紀元前1100年ごろには[[こぐま座ベータ星|こぐま座β星]]の北極距離が約6.5度能田忠亮 『東洋天文学史論叢』 恒星社、1943年、105頁。と、天の北極に最も近い[[北極星]]であり、'''帝'''と呼ばれていた。 そして、信仰の対象とは別に'''天皇大帝'''という名の星座も存在した。『開元占経』 の「巻69 甘氏中官占」 に見える大崎正次 （1987） 48頁。。『[[晋書]]』 「天文志」 には 「口中一星を天皇大帝と曰（い）ふ」 という記述がある。この 「口中」 は、天帝の後宮で天帝の住まう[[紫微垣|紫微宮]]（しびきゅう）を護衛する大崎正次 （1987） 153頁。'''勾陳'''（こうちん）という星座で、その第二星（こぐま座δ星、4等）・第一星（同α星・ポラリス、2等）・第五星（[[ケフェウス座]] [https://simbad.u-strasbg.fr/simbad/sim-id?protocol=html&Ident=HD5848 HD5848]、4等）・第六星（同 [https://simbad.u-strasbg.fr/simbad/sim-id?protocol=html&Ident=HD217382 HD217382]、5等）で描かれる四辺形のことを指しており、天皇大帝はその中にある5等星（同 [https://simbad.u-strasbg.fr/simbad/sim-id?protocol=html&Ident=HD212710 HD212710]）であるこれらの星の現行星との同定は 『欽定 儀象考成』 所載の[[星表]]のデータに対して土橋・シュヴァリエおよび伊が行ったものである。（大崎正次 （1987） 297頁。）。『和漢三才図絵』 でも天皇大帝は 「口」 の中に当たる位置に記されているが、この図はかなり不正確なので注意が必要である。なお、オランダの東洋学者[[グスタフ・シュレーゲル|シュレーゲル]]は、こぐま座α星を天皇大帝と同定している飯島忠夫 補訂 『支那古代史論』、恒星社厚生閣、1941年、第1圖。。ただし、大崎によれば、シュレーゲルによる同定は 「第一級の資料とは認められない」 とのことである大崎正次 （1987） 295頁。。 == 信仰 == 北斗七星（[[おおぐま座]]）は水を汲む 「斗」 の形をしており、大地を潤す農耕の神のシンボルでもあった。『[[史記]]』 『星経』 には北斗は北辰を中心に一晩で一回転し、一年で斗柄は十二方位を指し、止まることのない永久時計として陰陽（太陽と月のこと）、そして夏・冬を分け、農耕の作業時期を示し、国家安寧を保証するとある 『[[史記]]』 「天官書」 および 『星経』 の 「北斗」 の項を参照。。 天皇大帝はその聖性の象徴として神器（道教の用語）を持っている。神器は 「鏡」 と 「剣」 であり、呪具（magic tool）と威儀具とを兼ねている。 儒教では、中間色である 「紫」 を正色（[[原色]]）である 「赤」 よりも格下に見る たとえば 『[[論語]]』 「陽貨篇」 に孔子の言葉として 「紫の朱を奪うを悪（にく）む」 とある。が、天皇大帝は 「紫宮」 あるいは 「紫微宮」、「[[大内裏#大内裏の主な施設|紫宸殿]]」（ししんでん）、「[[大極殿]]」（だいごくでん）などと呼ばれる宮殿に住んでいることになっており、また北極星の光芒は紫色とされ、紫色を最高の神聖な色としているただし、当時の北極星とされていた帝星（こぐま座β星）は[[スペクトル分類|スペクトル型]]K型を示すので、赤っぽく見える。 日本の天皇家の五色之幡（ごしきのばん）の色彩観念およびその用語そのものも道教の 『墉城集仙録』（ようじょうしゅうせんろく）の 「九天玄女」 の伝記に見える。五色之幡は現在でも形を変えて神社や寺院、能の舞台などに見られる五色の幔幕である。[[伊勢神宮]]では社殿の建築材料や心の御柱（しんのみはしら）を伐採する際には「五色の薄絁（うすきぬ）」を用いると 『[[皇太神宮儀式帳]]』（804年）にあるが、道教の経典のひとつの 『[[抱朴子]]』（ほうぼくし）「登渉篇」 にも山中の石に敷いて祈るべきことが述べられ、他の道教の儀式にも頻出する。。 == 読み方 == [[諸橋轍次]] 『[[大漢和辞典]]』（大修館書店、1956年）に拠れば、[[歴史的仮名遣い]]は 「てんわうだいてい」 となる。発音は[[連声]]により 「テンノーダイテー」。なお同書の日本の 「天皇」 の読みは 「てんくわう」・「てんわう」 のみ。「てんわう」 での立項が標準である。 星座名としては、通例 「てんこうたいてい」 と振って 「テンコータイテー」 と読む大崎正次 （1987） 173頁。。ただし、鈴木駿太郎は振り仮名を振っていないが、索引では 「天乳」（てんにゅう）と 「天馬座」（てんまざ）の間に配置しているところから 「てんのう……」 と読んでいる鈴木駿太郎 『星の事典』 恒星社厚生閣、1968年、320頁 == 私幣禁断に類似する禁令 == 私幣禁断とは、一般には皇室の[[祖霊]]を祀る伊勢神宮を天皇・[[皇后]]・[[皇太子]]以外が祀ることを禁じたことを言う。これに似た内容の禁令が以下のように出されている。 *[[796年]]、[[天皇]]は[[北斗七星]]を祀ることを禁じた。罰則として 「法師は名を綱所に送り、俗人は違勅の罪に処せ」 と規定した（『[[類聚国史]]』 「延暦十五年」）。 *[[799年]]、[[斎宮]]が伊勢神宮へ行くに際して 「京畿の百姓」 に 「北辰日本においては、少なくとも[[江戸時代]]以降、北辰は北極星（こぐま座α星）と同一視されていた。しかしこの頃は北極（これは星座のことを指している）の天樞（てんすう、きりん座 [https://simbad.u-strasbg.fr/simbad/sim-id?protocol=html&Ident=HD112028 HD112028]）が最も天の北極に近い時期であった。中国本土ではともかく、日本において5等星にすぎない天樞を北極星と見ていたとは考えにくい。これは以下の私幣禁断の事例にも言えることである。に灯火を奉る」 ことを禁じた（『[[日本後紀]]』 「延暦十八年九月」）。 *[[811年]]、斎宮が伊勢神宮へ行くに際して九月の一ヵ月間、「北辰を祭り、挙哀改葬等の事」 を禁じた（『[[日本後紀]]』 「弘仁二年九月一日」）。 *[[835年]]、斎宮が伊勢神宮へ行くに際して九月の一ヵ月間、「京畿」 での 「北辰に火を供えること」 を禁じた（『[[続日本後紀]]』 「承和二年八月二日」）。 *[[967年]]施行の 『[[延喜式]]』 は斎宮が伊勢神宮へ行くに際して 「九月一日より三十日まで、京畿内、伊勢、近江、等の国、北辰に奉灯し、哀を挙げ、葬を改むる」 ことを禁じた。 なお、[[1811年]]、伊勢神宮の私幣禁断は解かれたが、北極星および北斗七星の祭祀解禁の時期は不明である。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 注釈 === {{Notelist}} === 出典 === {{Reflist|2}} == 参考文献 == *大崎正次 『中国の星座の歴史』 雄山閣出版、1987年。 *窪 徳忠 『道教の神々』 [[平河出版社]]、1986年。 *[[福永光司]] 『道教と古代日本』 [[人文書院]]、1987年。 *福光光司・[[千田稔_(歴史地理学者)|千田稔]]・[[高橋徹]]{{要曖昧さ回避|date=2022年4月}} 『日本の道教遺跡を歩く』 [[朝日新聞社]]〈朝日選書〉、2003年。 *[[吉野裕子]] 『陰陽五行と日本の天皇』 人文書院、1998年。 *[https://simbad.u-strasbg.fr/simbad/ SIMBAD]: Query by identifiers - Centre de Données astronomiques de Strasbourg == 関連項目 == *[[北極星]] *[[北辰]] *[[妙見菩薩]] *[[紫微中台]] {{中国神話}} {{astro-stub}} {{China-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:てんこうたいてい}} [[Category:道教]] [[Category:中国の星座]] [[Category:四御]]

{{for|「天皇大帝」の諡号を贈られた人物|劉龑}} [[画像:天皇大帝.JPG|thumb|天皇大帝の像（岡山寿天宮、台湾）]] [[画像:Sterneamwalberla2.jpg|thumb|230px|right|北極星を中心として回転する星々]] [[画像:Draco constellation map.png|thumb|230px|right|こぐま座とりゅう座。現在の北極星は図中の「Polaris」]] '''天皇大帝'''（てんのうたいてい{{Cite book|和書|author=田辺三郎助|year=1991|title=日本の仏像大百科〈5巻〉：習合神・高僧|page=18|publisher=[[ぎょうせい]]|isbn=4-324-02117-1}}、てんのうだいてい）は'''神格化'''された[[北辰]]（[[天の北極]]）福島久雄 『孔子の見た星空』 [[大修館書店]]、1997年。のこと。後述するように、日本における'''[[天皇]]'''という称号の起源の有力な候補の一つと考えられている。[[北斗七星]]と混同されることもある。 == 概要 == 古代中国では地上からは天空のある一点を中心として星々が巡っているように見えることを知っており、そこを'''北辰'''と呼び（天の北極に該当する）、'''宇宙の中心'''と考えられていた。そして神格化され、[[道教]]や日本で使われる称号の[[天皇]]にも取り入れられたとする説がある。 中国の[[唐]]の[[高宗 (唐)|高宗]]は 「天皇」 と称し、死後は皇后の[[則天武后]]によって 「天皇大帝」 の[[諡]]（おくりな）が付けられた高宗の天皇号は、「一代限りの個人的な称号」であって、厳密な意味での君主号ではなく、天武天皇はその1年半前に即位し、天皇号を称している。[[遠山美都男]]『天皇と日本の起源「飛鳥の大王」の謎を解く』（[[講談社現代新書]]、2003年）pp.271-272.。これは日本の[[天武天皇]]による 「天皇」 の号の使用開始とほぼ同時期であるが、どちらが先であるかは研究者間でも結論が出ていない。 道教では最高神を「[[玉皇大帝]]」としており、これを[[月宿|星宿]]における[[紫微垣]]にある北極星西暦500年頃に地球の[[歳差]]のために[[天の北極]]が移動するため、北極星は[[こぐま座ベータ星|こぐま座のβ星]]から[[ポラリス (恒星)|現在の北極星（α星）]]に移っている。に同定して「'''[[北極紫微大帝]]'''」としていた。のちにこれに[[北斗七星]]を神格化した北斗信仰における 「'''[[北斗星君|北斗真君]]'''」とが[[習合]]し、さらに星を仏教における[[妙見菩薩]]に見立てた妙見信仰が生まれた。このため、現在においても「北極紫微大帝（北極大帝・紫微大帝）」 と 「北斗真君」とは、本来は別の神であるとして分ける場合と、同一視する場合とがある。 中国の[[皇帝]]や日本の[[皇室]]の北斗信仰にもこの同一視が見られる。中国の皇帝の祭服の左袖には北斗七星、右袖には[[織女]]これは、いわゆる 「[[ベガ|おりひめ]]」 そのものではなく、「おりひめ」 を含む星座'''織女三星'''のことである。がデザインされている。[[宮内庁]]所蔵の[[孝明天皇]]の礼服 即位の大礼、元旦の朝賀の際のもの。は背中の中央上部にも北斗七星が置かれている。ただし、織女はない。 == 星座（星官）としての天皇大帝 == [[Image:和漢三才図絵「北極紫微垣之図」部分・上下逆.JPG|thumb|230px|right|『[[和漢三才図会]]』 「北極[[紫微垣]]之図」（部分）

HD5848]、4等）・第六星（同 [https://simbad.u-strasbg.fr/simbad/sim-id?protocol=html&Ident=HD217382 HD217382]、5等）で描かれる四辺形のことを指しており、天皇大帝はその中にある5等星（同 [https://simbad.u-strasbg.fr/simbad/sim-id?protocol=html&Ident=HD212710 HD212710]）であるこれらの星の現行星との同定は 『欽定 儀象考成』 所載の[[星表]]のデータに対して土橋・シュヴァリエおよび伊が行ったものである。（大崎正次 （1987） 297頁。）。『和漢三才図絵』 でも天皇大帝は 「口」 == 北斗七星（[[おおぐま座]]）は水を汲む 「斗」 の形をしており、大地を潤す農耕の神のシンボルでもあった。『[[史記]]』 『星経』 には北斗は北辰を中心に一晩で一回転し、一年で斗柄は十二方位を指し、止まることのない永久時計として陰陽（太陽と月のこと）、そして夏・冬を分け、農耕の作業時期を示し、国家安寧を保証するとある 『[[史記]]』 「天官書」 および 『星経』 の 「北斗」 の項を参照。。 天皇大帝はその聖性の象徴として神器（道教の用語）を持っている。神器は 「鏡」 と 「剣」 であり、呪具（magic tool）と威儀具とを兼ねている。 儒教では、中間色である 「紫」 を正色（[[原色]]）である 「赤」 よりも格下に見る たとえば 『[[論語]]』 「陽貨篇」 に孔子の言葉として 「紫の朱を奪うを悪（にく）む」 とある。が、天皇大帝は 「紫宮」 あるいは 「紫微宮」、「[[大内裏#大内裏の主な施設|紫宸殿]]」（ししんでん）、「[[大極殿]]」（だいごくでん）などと呼ばれる宮殿に住んでいることになっており、また北極星の光芒は紫色とされ、紫色を最高の神聖な色としているただし、当時の北極星とされていた帝星（こぐま座β星）は[[スペクトル分類|スペクトル型]]K型を示すので、赤っぽく見える。 日本の天皇家の五色之幡（ごしきのばん）の色彩観念およびその用語そのものも道教の 『墉城集仙録』（ようじょうしゅうせんろく）の 「九天玄女」 の伝記に見える。五色之幡は現在でも形を変えて神社や寺院、能の舞台などに見られる五色の幔幕である。[[伊勢神宮]]では社殿の建築材料や心の御柱（しんのみはしら）を伐採する際には「五色の薄絁（うすきぬ）」を用いると 『[[皇太神宮儀式帳]]』（804年）にあるが、道教の経典のひとつの 『[[抱朴子]]』（ほうぼくし）「登渉篇」 にも山中の石に敷いて祈るべきことが述べられ、他の道教の儀式にも頻出する。。 == 読み方 == [[諸橋轍次]] 『[[大漢和辞典]]』（大修館書店、1956年）に拠れば、[[歴史的仮名遣い]]は 「てんわうだいてい」 となる。発音は[[連声]]により 「テンノーダイテー」。なお同書の日本の 「天皇」 の読みは 「てんくわう」・「てんわう」 のみ。「てんわう」 での立項が標準である。 星座名としては、通例 「てんこうたいてい」 と振って 「テンコータイテー」 と読む大崎正次 （1987） 173頁。。ただし、鈴木駿太郎は振り仮名を振っていないが、索引では 「天乳」（てんにゅう）と 「天馬座」（てんまざ）の間に配置しているところから 「てんのう……」 と読んでいる鈴木駿太郎 『星の事典』 恒星社厚生閣、1968年、320頁 == 私幣禁断に類似する禁令 == 私幣禁断とは、一般には皇室の[[祖霊]]を祀る伊勢神宮を天皇・[[皇后]]・[[皇太子]]以外が祀ることを禁じたことを言う。これに似た内容の禁令が以下のように出されている。 *[[796年]]、[[天皇]]は[[北斗七星]]を祀ることを禁じた。罰則として 「法師は名を綱所に送り、俗人は違勅の罪に処せ」 と規定した（『[[類聚国史]]』 「延暦十五年」）。 *[[799年]]、[[斎宮]]が伊勢神宮へ行くに際して 「京畿の百姓」 に 「北辰日本においては、少なくとも[[江戸時代]]以降、北辰は北極星（こぐま座α星）と同一視されていた。しかしこの頃は北極（これは星座のことを指している）の天樞（てんすう、きりん座 [https://simbad.u-strasbg.fr/simbad/sim-id?protocol=html&Ident=HD112028 HD112028]）が最も天の北極に近い時期であった。中国本土ではともかく、日本において5等星にすぎない天樞を北極星と見ていたとは考えにくい。これは以下の私幣禁断の事例にも言えることである。に灯火を奉る」 ことを禁じた（『[[日本後紀]]』 「延暦十八年九月」）。 *[[811年]]、斎宮が伊勢神宮へ行くに際して九月の一ヵ月間、「北辰を祭り、挙哀改葬等の事」 を禁じた（『[[日本後紀]]』 「弘仁二年九月一日」）。 *[[835年]]、斎宮が伊勢神宮へ行くに際して九月の一ヵ月間、「京畿」 での 「北辰に火を供えること」 を禁じた（『[[続日本後紀]]』 「承和二年八月二日」）。 *[[967年]]施行の 『[[延喜式]]』 は斎宮が伊勢神宮へ行くに際して 「九月一日より三十日まで、京畿内、伊勢、近江、等の国、北辰に奉灯し、哀を挙げ、葬を改むる」 ことを禁じた。 なお、[[1811年]]、伊勢神宮の私幣禁断は解かれたが、北極星および北斗七星の祭祀解禁の時期は不明である。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 注釈 === {{Notelist}} === 出典 === {{Reflist|2}} == 参考文献 == *大崎正次 『中国の星座の歴史』 雄山閣出版、1987年。 *窪 徳忠 『道教の神々』 [[平河出版社]]、1986年。 *[[福永光司]] 『道教と古代日本』 [[人文書院]]、1987年。 *福光光司・[[千田稔_(歴史地理学者)|千田稔]]・[[高橋徹]]{{要曖昧さ回避|date=2022年4月}} 『日本の道教遺跡を歩く』 [[朝日新聞社]]〈朝日選書〉、2003年。 *[[吉野裕子]] 『陰陽五行と日本の天皇』 人文書院、1998年。 *[https://simbad.u-strasbg.fr/simbad/ SIMBAD]: Query by identifiers - Centre de Données astronomiques de Strasbourg == 関連項目 == *[[北極星]] *[[北辰]] *[[妙見菩薩]] *[[紫微中台]] {{for|The figure given the posthumous title of "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor"|{{ill|Liu Yan (Southern Han)|ja|劉龑|qq=draft|qq=draft}}}} [[File:天皇大帝.JPG|thumb|Statue of the Heavenly Sovereign Emperor (Tianhuang Dadi) at Okayamajutenkyu, Taiwan]] [[File:Sterneamwalberla2.jpg|thumb|230px|right|Stars rotating around the North Star]] [[File:Draco constellation map.png|thumb|230px|right|Ursa Minor and Draco. The current North Star is marked as "Polaris"]]
'''Heavenly Sovereign Emperor''' (天皇大帝, ''Ten'nō Taitei''<ref>{{Cite book|author=Saburōsuke Tanabe|year=1991|title=Comprehensive Encyclopedia of Japanese Buddhist Statues, Volume 5: Syncretic Deities and Eminent Monks|page=18|publisher={{ill|Gyosei|ja|ぎょうせい|qq=draft|qq=draft}}|isbn=4-324-02117-1}}</ref>) refers to the deification of the '''Northern Celestial Pole''' (北辰, ''Hokushin'')<ref>Hisao Fukushima, ''The Night Sky as Seen by Confucius,'' {{ill|Taishukan Shoten|ja|大修館書店|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, 1997.</ref>. It is considered one of the most prominent theories regarding the origin of the title '''{{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' in Japan. It has sometimes been conflated with the [[Big Dipper]] due to the movement of the celestial pole.
[[Emperor Gaozong of Tang]] was named such and lived from 21 July 628<ref name="birth">According to Tang Taizong's biography in the ''[[Old Book of Tang]]'', Li Zhi was born on the ''gengyin'' day in the 6th month of the 2nd year of the ''Zhen'guan'' era of Taizong's reign. This corresponds to 21 Jul 628 in the Gregorian calendar. [(貞觀二年)六月庚寅，皇子治生] ''Old Book of Tang, vol. 2''</ref> – 27 December 683<ref name="death">Volume 203 of ''[[Zizhi Tongjian]]'' recorded that Gaozong died in the night of the ''dingsi'' day of the 12th month of the 1st year of the Hongdao era of his reign. This date corresponds to 27 Dec 683 in the Gregorian calendar. [(弘道元年)十二月，丁巳，...。是夜，....，上崩於貞觀殿。] ''Zizhi Tongjian'', vol.203.</ref>. The term Tenno (天皇) was first attested in 720 with the [[Nihon Shoki]] so this god or the title is attributed as a possible source of the title.
== Overview == Ancient China understood that stars appeared to rotate around a fixed point in the sky, which was called the '''Northern Celestial Pole''' (corresponding to the celestial north pole) and regarded as the '''center of the universe'''. This belief led to its deification and its incorporation into the titles used in {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and later in Japan’s imperial system.
During the Tang dynasty, {{ill|Emperor Gaozong of Tang|ja|高宗 (唐)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} took the title of "Heavenly Sovereign" (天皇), and after his death, his consort {{ill|Wu Zetian|lt=Empress Wu Zetian|ja|則天武后|qq=draft|qq=draft}} granted him the posthumous title "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor" (天皇大帝)<ref>Gaozong’s use of the title "Heavenly Sovereign" was a "one-generation personal title" rather than a strict monarchic title. This occurred about a year and a half after {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft}} had begun using the title in Japan. See {{ill|Mitsuo Toyama|ja|遠山美都男|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, ''The Origins of the Emperor and Japan: Unraveling the Mystery of the "Great Kings of Asuka,"'' Kodansha Gendai Shinsho, 2003, pp. 271-272.</ref>. This coincided with the early use of the "Emperor" (天皇) title in Japan under {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, though scholars debate which came first.
Taoism venerates the supreme deity as {{ill|Jade Emperor|ja|玉皇大帝|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, associating him with the [[:en:Ziwei_enclosure|purple forbidden enclosure]] and the {{ill|North Star|ja|北極星|qq=draft|qq=draft}}<ref group="note">Due to Earth’s precession, the celestial north pole shifted around 500 CE, transitioning from {{ill|Beta Ursae Minoris|lt=Kochab|ja|こぐま座ベータ星|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to {{ill|Polaris|ja|ポラリス (恒星)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.</ref>. The deity was identified as '''{{ill|Ziwei Emperor|lt=Beiji Ziwei Dadi|ja|北極紫微大帝|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'''. This belief later syncretized with the {{ill|Big Dipper Faith|ja|北斗信仰|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, deifying the Big Dipper as '''{{ill|Beidou Zhenjun|ja|北斗星君|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'''. In time, it further merged with the Buddhist concept of {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, forming the '''Myōken faith'''. Consequently, "Beiji Ziwei Dadi" (North Pole Purple Tenuous Emperor) and "Beidou Zhenjun" (Big Dipper True Lord) are sometimes considered distinct and sometimes identified as the same deity.
The belief in the North Star's divine power extended to Chinese emperors and Japan’s imperial family. The robes of Chinese emperors featured the {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星|qq=draft|qq=draft}} on the left sleeve and the {{ill|Zhinü|lt=Weaving Maiden|ja|織女|qq=draft|qq=draft}}<ref group="note">This does not refer to {{ill|Vega|ja|ベガ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} alone but to the constellation {{ill|Weaving Maiden Three Stars|ja|織女三星|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which includes Vega.</ref> on the right. Meanwhile, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁|qq=draft|qq=draft}} preserves an imperial robe of {{ill|Emperor Komei|ja|孝明天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, with the Big Dipper embroidered at the center of the upper back, though without the Weaving Maiden.
== The Heavenly Sovereign Emperor as a Constellation == [[File:和漢三才図絵「北極紫微垣之図」部分・上下逆.JPG|thumb|230px|right|''{{ill|Wakan Sansai Zue|ja|和漢三才図会|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'': "Map of the Purple Forbidden Enclosure" with ''Beiji Ziwei Dadi'' (北極紫微大帝) and ''Heavenly Sovereign Emperor'' (天皇大帝)]]
Beyond its religious significance, "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor" also referred to an actual asterism. It is mentioned in ''{{ill|Kaiyuan Zhanjing|ja|開元占経|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'', Volume 69, "Ganshi's Central Observations" (甘氏中官占)<ref>Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 48.</ref>. In the ''{{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'', ''Tianwen Zhi'' (Astronomical Records) states: "Within the 'mouth' is one star called 'Heavenly Sovereign Emperor'" (口中一星を天皇大帝と曰ふ).
This "mouth" refers to the asterism ''Gouchen'' (勾陳), which guarded the celestial palace of the Jade Emperor, the {{ill|Purple Forbidden Enclosure|ja|紫微垣|qq=draft|qq=draft}}<ref>Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 153.</ref>. The star known as "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor" is believed to be {{ill|HD212710|ja|HD212710|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a 5th magnitude star in {{ill|Cepheus|ja|ケフェウス座|qq=draft|qq=draft}}<ref group="note">Modern identifications were made using the star catalog data from ''Imperial Astronomical Treatise'' (欽定儀象考成) by Tsuchihashi, Chevalier, and Iida. See Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 297.</ref>.
== Worship and Rituals == The {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was seen as a celestial ladle pouring cosmic blessings. The ''{{ill|Records of the Grand Historian|ja|史記|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'' describes the Big Dipper as revolving around the North Star, marking time, seasons, and ensuring the stability of the state<ref>''Shiji'' "Treatise on Astronomy" and ''Star Canon'' (星経), "The Big Dipper" section.</ref>.
The Heavenly Sovereign Emperor is associated with divine regalia, specifically a '''mirror''' and a '''sword'''. These items serve as both ritual implements and symbols of authority.
== Reading and Pronunciation == According to {{ill|Tetsuji Morohashi|ja|諸橋轍次|qq=draft|qq=draft}}’s ''{{ill|Dai Kan-Wa Jiten|ja|大漢和辞典|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'' (1956), the historical reading of "天皇大帝" was ''Ten'wō Taitei'' (てんわうだいてい), pronounced as ''Tennō Daitei'' due to {{ill|Rendaku|ja|連声|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. As a constellation name, it was commonly read as ''Tenkō Taitei'' (てんこうたいてい)<ref>Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 173.</ref>. == See Also == * {{ill|North Star|ja|北極星|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Northern Celestial Pole|ja|北辰|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * [[:en:Ziwei_enclosure|purple forbidden enclosure]]

See Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 297.</ref>.
== Worship and Rituals == The {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was seen as a celestial ladle pouring cosmic blessings. The ''{{ill|Records of the Grand Historian|ja|史記|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'' describes the Big Dipper as revolving around the North Star, marking time, seasons, and ensuring the stability of the state<ref>''Shiji'' "Treatise on Astronomy" and ''Star Canon'' (星経), "The Big Dipper" section.</ref>.
The Heavenly Sovereign Emperor is associated with divine regalia, specifically a '''mirror''' and a '''sword'''. These items serve as both ritual implements and symbols of authority.
== Reading and Pronunciation == According to {{ill|Tetsuji Morohashi|ja|諸橋轍次|qq=draft|qq=draft}}’s ''{{ill|Dai Kan-Wa Jiten|ja|大漢和辞典|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'' (1956), the historical reading of "天皇大帝" was ''Ten'wō Taitei'' (てんわうだいてい), pronounced as ''Tennō Daitei'' due to {{ill|Rendaku|ja|連声|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. As a constellation name, it was commonly read as ''Tenkō Taitei'' (てんこうたいてい)<ref>Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 173.</ref>. == See Also == * {{ill|North Star|ja|北極星|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Northern Celestial Pole|ja|北辰|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * [[:en:Ziwei_enclosure|purple forbidden enclosure]]
==Notes== <references group="note"/>
==References==
