{{draft categories| [[Category:Taoism]] [[Category:Chinese constellations]] [[Category:Four Sovereigns]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Four heavenly ministers]] [[Category:Expanded pages from Wikipedia]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tianhuang Emperor]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tianhuang Emperor]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tianhuang Emperor]][[Category:qq]] }}
[[sn:Tianhuang Emperor]] [[ja:天皇大帝]] [[en:Tianhuang Emperor]]

{{for|The figure given the posthumous title of "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor"|{{ill|Liu Yan (Southern Han)|ja|劉龑|qq=draft|qq=draft}}}} [[File:天皇大帝.JPG|thumb|Statue of the Heavenly Sovereign Emperor (Tianhuang Dadi) at Okayamajutenkyu, Taiwan]] [[File:Sterneamwalberla2.jpg|thumb|230px|right|Stars rotating around the North Star]] [[File:Draco constellation map.png|thumb|230px|right|Ursa Minor and Draco. The current North Star is marked as "Polaris"]]
'''Heavenly Sovereign Emperor''' (天皇大帝, ''Ten'nō Taitei''<ref>{{Cite book|author=Saburōsuke Tanabe|year=1991|title=Comprehensive Encyclopedia of Japanese Buddhist Statues, Volume 5: Syncretic Deities and Eminent Monks|page=18|publisher={{ill|Gyosei|ja|ぎょうせい|qq=draft|qq=draft}}|isbn=4-324-02117-1}}</ref>) refers to the deification of the '''Northern Celestial Pole''' (北辰, ''Hokushin'')<ref>Hisao Fukushima, ''The Night Sky as Seen by Confucius,'' {{ill|Taishukan Shoten|ja|大修館書店|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, 1997.</ref>. It is considered one of the most prominent theories regarding the origin of the title '''{{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' in Japan. It has sometimes been conflated with the [[Big Dipper]] due to the movement of the celestial pole.
[[Emperor Gaozong of Tang]] was named such and lived from 21 July 628<ref name="birth">According to Tang Taizong's biography in the ''[[Old Book of Tang]]'', Li Zhi was born on the ''gengyin'' day in the 6th month of the 2nd year of the ''Zhen'guan'' era of Taizong's reign. This corresponds to 21 Jul 628 in the Gregorian calendar. [(貞觀二年)六月庚寅，皇子治生] ''Old Book of Tang, vol. 2''</ref> – 27 December 683<ref name="death">Volume 203 of ''[[Zizhi Tongjian]]'' recorded that Gaozong died in the night of the ''dingsi'' day of the 12th month of the 1st year of the Hongdao era of his reign. This date corresponds to 27 Dec 683 in the Gregorian calendar. [(弘道元年)十二月，丁巳，...。是夜，....，上崩於貞觀殿。] ''Zizhi Tongjian'', vol.203.</ref>. The term Tenno (天皇) was first attested in 720 with the [[Nihon Shoki]] so this god or the title is attributed as a possible source of the title.
== Overview == Ancient China understood that stars appeared to rotate around a fixed point in the sky, which was called the '''Northern Celestial Pole''' (corresponding to the celestial north pole) and regarded as the '''center of the universe'''. This belief led to its deification and its incorporation into the titles used in {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and later in Japan’s imperial system.
During the Tang dynasty, {{ill|Emperor Gaozong of Tang|ja|高宗 (唐)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} took the title of "Heavenly Sovereign" (天皇), and after his death, his consort {{ill|Wu Zetian|lt=Empress Wu Zetian|ja|則天武后|qq=draft|qq=draft}} granted him the posthumous title "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor" (天皇大帝)<ref>Gaozong’s use of the title "Heavenly Sovereign" was a "one-generation personal title" rather than a strict monarchic title. This occurred about a year and a half after {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft}} had begun using the title in Japan. See {{ill|Mitsuo Toyama|ja|遠山美都男|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, ''The Origins of the Emperor and Japan: Unraveling the Mystery of the "Great Kings of Asuka,"'' Kodansha Gendai Shinsho, 2003, pp. 271-272.</ref>. This coincided with the early use of the "Emperor" (天皇) title in Japan under {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, though scholars debate which came first.
Taoism venerates the supreme deity as {{ill|Jade Emperor|ja|玉皇大帝|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, associating him with the [[:en:Ziwei_enclosure|purple forbidden enclosure]] and the {{ill|North Star|ja|北極星|qq=draft|qq=draft}}<ref group="note">Due to Earth’s precession, the celestial north pole shifted around 500 CE, transitioning from {{ill|Beta Ursae Minoris|lt=Kochab|ja|こぐま座ベータ星|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to {{ill|Polaris|ja|ポラリス (恒星)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.</ref>. The deity was identified as '''{{ill|Ziwei Emperor|lt=Beiji Ziwei Dadi|ja|北極紫微大帝|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'''. This belief later syncretized with the {{ill|Big Dipper Faith|ja|北斗信仰|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, deifying the Big Dipper as '''{{ill|Beidou Zhenjun|ja|北斗星君|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'''. In time, it further merged with the Buddhist concept of {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, forming the '''Myōken faith'''. Consequently, "Beiji Ziwei Dadi" (North Pole Purple Tenuous Emperor) and "Beidou Zhenjun" (Big Dipper True Lord) are sometimes considered distinct and sometimes identified as the same deity.
The belief in the North Star's divine power extended to Chinese emperors and Japan’s imperial family. The robes of Chinese emperors featured the {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星|qq=draft|qq=draft}} on the left sleeve and the {{ill|Zhinü|lt=Weaving Maiden|ja|織女|qq=draft|qq=draft}}<ref group="note">This does not refer to {{ill|Vega|ja|ベガ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} alone but to the constellation {{ill|Weaving Maiden Three Stars|ja|織女三星|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which includes Vega.</ref> on the right. Meanwhile, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁|qq=draft|qq=draft}} preserves an imperial robe of {{ill|Emperor Komei|ja|孝明天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, with the Big Dipper embroidered at the center of the upper back, though without the Weaving Maiden.
== The Heavenly Sovereign Emperor as a Constellation == [[File:和漢三才図絵「北極紫微垣之図」部分・上下逆.JPG|thumb|230px|right|''{{ill|Wakan Sansai Zue|ja|和漢三才図会|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'': "Map of the Purple Forbidden Enclosure" with ''Beiji Ziwei Dadi'' (北極紫微大帝) and ''Heavenly Sovereign Emperor'' (天皇大帝)]]
Beyond its religious significance, "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor" also referred to an actual asterism. It is mentioned in ''{{ill|Kaiyuan Zhanjing|ja|開元占経|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'', Volume 69, "Ganshi's Central Observations" (甘氏中官占)<ref>Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 48.</ref>. In the ''{{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'', ''Tianwen Zhi'' (Astronomical Records) states: "Within the 'mouth' is one star called 'Heavenly Sovereign Emperor'" (口中一星を天皇大帝と曰ふ).
This "mouth" refers to the asterism ''Gouchen'' (勾陳), which guarded the celestial palace of the Jade Emperor, the {{ill|Purple Forbidden Enclosure|ja|紫微垣|qq=draft|qq=draft}}<ref>Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 153.</ref>. The star known as "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor" is believed to be {{ill|HD212710|ja|HD212710|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a 5th magnitude star in {{ill|Cepheus|ja|ケフェウス座|qq=draft|qq=draft}}<ref group="note">Modern identifications were made using the star catalog data from ''Imperial Astronomical Treatise'' (欽定儀象考成) by Tsuchihashi, Chevalier, and Iida. See Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 297.</ref>.
== Worship and Rituals == The {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was seen as a celestial ladle pouring cosmic blessings. The ''{{ill|Records of the Grand Historian|ja|史記|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'' describes the Big Dipper as revolving around the North Star, marking time, seasons, and ensuring the stability of the state<ref>''Shiji'' "Treatise on Astronomy" and ''Star Canon'' (星経), "The Big Dipper" section.</ref>.
The Heavenly Sovereign Emperor is associated with divine regalia, specifically a '''mirror''' and a '''sword'''. These items serve as both ritual implements and symbols of authority.
== Reading and Pronunciation == According to {{ill|Tetsuji Morohashi|ja|諸橋轍次|qq=draft|qq=draft}}’s ''{{ill|Dai Kan-Wa Jiten|ja|大漢和辞典|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'' (1956), the historical reading of "天皇大帝" was ''Ten'wō Taitei'' (てんわうだいてい), pronounced as ''Tennō Daitei'' due to {{ill|Rendaku|ja|連声|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. As a constellation name, it was commonly read as ''Tenkō Taitei'' (てんこうたいてい)<ref>Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 173.</ref>. == See Also == * {{ill|North Star|ja|北極星|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Northern Celestial Pole|ja|北辰|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * [[:en:Ziwei_enclosure|purple forbidden enclosure]]
==Notes== <references group="note"/>
==References==

This occurred about a year and a half after {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=}} had begun using the title in Japan. See {{ill|Mitsuo Toyama|ja|遠山美都男|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsuo Toyama|qq=}}, ''The Origins of the Emperor and Japan: Unraveling the Mystery of the "Great Kings of Asuka,"'' Kodansha Gendai Shinsho, 2003, pp. 271-272.</ref>. This coincided with the early use of the "Emperor" (天皇) title in Japan under {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=}}, though scholars debate which came first.
Taoism venerates the supreme deity as {{ill|Jade Emperor|ja|玉皇大帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jade Emperor|qq=}}, associating him with the [[:en:Ziwei_enclosure|purple forbidden enclosure]] and the {{ill|North Star|ja|北極星|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/North Star|qq=}}<ref group="note">Due to Earth’s precession, the celestial north pole shifted around 500 CE, transitioning from {{ill|Beta Ursae Minoris|lt=Kochab|ja|こぐま座ベータ星|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beta Ursae Minoris|qq=}} to {{ill|Polaris|ja|ポラリス (恒星)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Polaris|qq=}}.</ref>. The deity was identified as '''{{ill|Ziwei Emperor|lt=Beiji Ziwei Dadi|ja|北極紫微大帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ziwei Emperor|qq=}}'''. This belief later syncretized with the {{ill|Big Dipper Faith|ja|北斗信仰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Big Dipper Faith|qq=}}, deifying the Big Dipper as '''{{ill|Beidou Zhenjun|ja|北斗星君|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beidou Zhenjun|qq=}}'''. In time, it further merged with the Buddhist concept of {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōken Bosatsu|qq=}}, forming the '''Myōken faith'''. Consequently, "Beiji Ziwei Dadi" (North Pole Purple Tenuous Emperor) and "Beidou Zhenjun" (Big Dipper True Lord) are sometimes considered distinct and sometimes identified as the same deity.
The belief in the North Star's divine power extended to Chinese emperors and Japan’s imperial family. The robes of Chinese emperors featured the {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Big Dipper|qq=}} on the left sleeve and the {{ill|Zhinü|lt=Weaving Maiden|ja|織女|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhinü|qq=}}<ref group="note">This does not refer to {{ill|Vega|ja|ベガ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Vega|qq=}} alone but to the constellation {{ill|Weaving Maiden Three Stars|ja|織女三星|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Weaving Maiden Three Stars|qq=}}, which includes Vega.</ref> on the right. Meanwhile, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Agency|qq=}} preserves an imperial robe of {{ill|Emperor Komei|ja|孝明天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Komei|qq=}}, with the Big Dipper embroidered at the center of the upper back, though without the Weaving Maiden.
== The Heavenly Sovereign Emperor as a Constellation == [[File:和漢三才図絵「北極紫微垣之図」部分・上下逆.JPG|thumb|230px|right|''{{ill|Wakan Sansai Zue|ja|和漢三才図会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakan Sansai Zue|qq=}}'': "Map of the Purple Forbidden Enclosure" with ''Beiji Ziwei Dadi'' (北極紫微大帝) and ''Heavenly Sovereign Emperor'' (天皇大帝)]]
Beyond its religious significance, "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor" also referred to an actual asterism. It is mentioned in ''{{ill|Kaiyuan Zhanjing|ja|開元占経|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaiyuan Zhanjing|qq=}}'', Volume 69, "Ganshi's Central Observations" (甘氏中官占)<ref>Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 48.</ref>. In the ''{{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Jin|qq=}}'', ''Tianwen Zhi'' (Astronomical Records) states: "Within the 'mouth' is one star called 'Heavenly Sovereign Emperor'" (口中一星を天皇大帝と曰ふ).
This "mouth" refers to the asterism ''Gouchen'' (勾陳), which guarded the celestial palace of the Jade Emperor, the {{ill|Purple Forbidden Enclosure|ja|紫微垣|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Purple Forbidden Enclosure|qq=}}<ref>Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 153.</ref>. The star known as "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor" is believed to be {{ill|HD212710|ja|HD212710|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/HD212710|qq=}}, a 5th magnitude star in {{ill|Cepheus|ja|ケフェウス座|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cepheus|qq=}}<ref group="note">Modern identifications were made using the star catalog data from ''Imperial Astronomical Treatise'' (欽定儀象考成) by Tsuchihashi, Chevalier, and Iida. See Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 297.</ref>.
== Worship and Rituals == The {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Big Dipper|qq=}} was seen as a celestial ladle pouring cosmic blessings. The ''{{ill|Records of the Grand Historian|ja|史記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Records of the Grand Historian|qq=}}'' describes the Big Dipper as revolving around the North Star, marking time, seasons, and ensuring the stability of the state<ref>''Shiji'' "Treatise on Astronomy" and ''Star Canon'' (星経), "The Big Dipper" section.</ref>.
The Heavenly Sovereign Emperor is associated with divine regalia, specifically a '''mirror''' and a '''sword'''. These items serve as both ritual implements and symbols of authority.
== Reading and Pronunciation == According to {{ill|Tetsuji Morohashi|ja|諸橋轍次|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tetsuji Morohashi|qq=}}’s ''{{ill|Dai Kan-Wa Jiten|ja|大漢和辞典|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dai Kan-Wa Jiten|qq=}}'' (1956), the historical reading of "天皇大帝" was ''Ten'wō Taitei'' (てんわうだいてい), pronounced as ''Tennō Daitei'' due to {{ill|Rendaku|ja|連声|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rendaku|qq=}}. As a constellation name, it was commonly read as ''Tenkō Taitei'' (てんこうたいてい)<ref>Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 173.</ref>. == See Also == * {{ill|North Star|ja|北極星|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/North Star|qq=}} * {{ill|Northern Celestial Pole|ja|北辰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Northern Celestial Pole|qq=}} * {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōken Bosatsu|qq=}} * [[:en:Ziwei_enclosure|purple forbidden enclosure]]
==Notes== <references group="note"/>

[[sn:Tianhuang Emperor]] [[ja:天皇大帝]] [[en:Tianhuang Emperor]]

revision.text:
{{for|The figure given the posthumous title of "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor"|{{ill|Liu Yan (Southern Han)|ja|劉龑|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Liu Yan (Southern Han)|qq=}}}} [[File:天皇大帝.JPG|thumb|Statue of the Heavenly Sovereign Emperor (Tianhuang Dadi) at Okayamajutenkyu, Taiwan]] [[File:Sterneamwalberla2.jpg|thumb|230px|right|Stars rotating around the North Star]] [[File:Draco constellation map.png|thumb|230px|right|Ursa Minor and Draco. The current North Star is marked as "Polaris"]]
'''Heavenly Sovereign Emperor''' (天皇大帝, ''Ten'nō Taitei''<ref>{{Cite book|author=Saburōsuke Tanabe|year=1991|title=Comprehensive Encyclopedia of Japanese Buddhist Statues, Volume 5: Syncretic Deities and Eminent Monks|page=18|publisher={{ill|Gyosei|ja|ぎょうせい|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyosei|qq=}}|isbn=4-324-02117-1}}</ref>) refers to the deification of the '''Northern Celestial Pole''' (北辰, ''Hokushin'')<ref>Hisao Fukushima, ''The Night Sky as Seen by Confucius,'' {{ill|Taishukan Shoten|ja|大修館書店|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishukan Shoten|qq=}}, 1997.</ref>. It is considered one of the most prominent theories regarding the origin of the title '''{{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor of Japan|qq=}}''' in Japan. It has sometimes been conflated with the [[Big Dipper]] due to the movement of the celestial pole.
[[Emperor Gaozong of Tang]] was named such and lived from 21 July 628<ref name="birth">According to Tang Taizong's biography in the ''[[Old Book of Tang]]'', Li Zhi was born on the ''gengyin'' day in the 6th month of the 2nd year of the ''Zhen'guan'' era of Taizong's reign. This corresponds to 21 Jul 628 in the Gregorian calendar. [(貞觀二年)六月庚寅，皇子治生] ''Old Book of Tang, vol. 2''</ref> – 27 December 683<ref name="death">Volume 203 of ''[[Zizhi Tongjian]]'' recorded that Gaozong died in the night of the ''dingsi'' day of the 12th month of the 1st year of the Hongdao era of his reign. This date corresponds to 27 Dec 683 in the Gregorian calendar. [(弘道元年)十二月，丁巳，...。是夜，....，上崩於貞觀殿。] ''Zizhi Tongjian'', vol.203.</ref>. The term Tenno (天皇) was first attested in 720 with the [[Nihon Shoki]] so this god or the title is attributed as a possible source of the title.
== Overview == Ancient China understood that stars appeared to rotate around a fixed point in the sky, which was called the '''Northern Celestial Pole''' (corresponding to the celestial north pole) and regarded as the '''center of the universe'''. This belief led to its deification and its incorporation into the titles used in {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taoism|qq=}} and later in Japan’s imperial system.
During the Tang dynasty, {{ill|Emperor Gaozong of Tang|ja|高宗 (唐)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Gaozong of Tang|qq=}} took the title of "Heavenly Sovereign" (天皇), and after his death, his consort {{ill|Wu Zetian|lt=Empress Wu Zetian|ja|則天武后|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wu Zetian|qq=}} granted him the posthumous title "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor" (天皇大帝)<ref>Gaozong’s use of the title "Heavenly Sovereign" was a "one-generation personal title" rather than a strict monarchic title. This occurred about a year and a half after {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=}} had begun using the title in Japan. See {{ill|Mitsuo Toyama|ja|遠山美都男|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsuo Toyama|qq=}}, ''The Origins of the Emperor and Japan: Unraveling the Mystery of the "Great Kings of Asuka,"'' Kodansha Gendai Shinsho, 2003, pp. 271-272.</ref>. This coincided with the early use of the "Emperor" (天皇) title in Japan under {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=}}, though scholars debate which came first.
Taoism venerates the supreme deity as {{ill|Jade Emperor|ja|玉皇大帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jade Emperor|qq=}}, associating him with the [[:en:Ziwei_enclosure|purple forbidden enclosure]] and the {{ill|North Star|ja|北極星|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/North Star|qq=}}<ref group="note">Due to Earth’s precession, the celestial north pole shifted around 500 CE, transitioning from {{ill|Beta Ursae Minoris|lt=Kochab|ja|こぐま座ベータ星|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beta Ursae Minoris|qq=}} to {{ill|Polaris|ja|ポラリス (恒星)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Polaris|qq=}}.</ref>. The deity was identified as '''{{ill|Ziwei Emperor|lt=Beiji Ziwei Dadi|ja|北極紫微大帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ziwei Emperor|qq=}}'''. This belief later syncretized with the {{ill|Big Dipper Faith|ja|北斗信仰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Big Dipper Faith|qq=}}, deifying the Big Dipper as '''{{ill|Beidou Zhenjun|ja|北斗星君|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beidou Zhenjun|qq=}}'''. In time, it further merged with the Buddhist concept of {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōken Bosatsu|qq=}}, forming the '''Myōken faith'''. Consequently, "Beiji Ziwei Dadi" (North Pole Purple Tenuous Emperor) and "Beidou Zhenjun" (Big Dipper True Lord) are sometimes considered distinct and sometimes identified as the same deity.
The belief in the North Star's divine power extended to Chinese emperors and Japan’s imperial family. The robes of Chinese emperors featured the {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Big Dipper|qq=}} on the left sleeve and the {{ill|Zhinü|lt=Weaving Maiden|ja|織女|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhinü|qq=}}<ref group="note">This does not refer to {{ill|Vega|ja|ベガ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Vega|qq=}} alone but to the constellation {{ill|Weaving Maiden Three Stars|ja|織女三星|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Weaving Maiden Three Stars|qq=}}, which includes Vega.</ref> on the right. Meanwhile, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Agency|qq=}} preserves an imperial robe of {{ill|Emperor Komei|ja|孝明天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Komei|qq=}}, with the Big Dipper embroidered at the center of the upper back, though without the Weaving Maiden.
== The Heavenly Sovereign Emperor as a Constellation == [[File:和漢三才図絵「北極紫微垣之図」部分・上下逆.JPG|thumb|230px|right|''{{ill|Wakan Sansai Zue|ja|和漢三才図会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakan Sansai Zue|qq=}}'': "Map of the Purple Forbidden Enclosure" with ''Beiji Ziwei Dadi'' (北極紫微大帝) and ''Heavenly Sovereign Emperor'' (天皇大帝)]]
Beyond its religious significance, "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor" also referred to an actual asterism. It is mentioned in ''{{ill|Kaiyuan Zhanjing|ja|開元占経|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaiyuan Zhanjing|qq=}}'', Volume 69, "Ganshi's Central Observations" (甘氏中官占)<ref>Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 48.</ref>. In the ''{{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Jin|qq=}}'', ''Tianwen Zhi'' (Astronomical Records) states: "Within the 'mouth' is one star called 'Heavenly Sovereign Emperor'" (口中一星を天皇大帝と曰ふ).
This "mouth" refers to the asterism ''Gouchen'' (勾陳), which guarded the celestial palace of the Jade Emperor, the {{ill|Purple Forbidden Enclosure|ja|紫微垣|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Purple Forbidden Enclosure|qq=}}<ref>Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 153.</ref>. The star known as "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor" is believed to be {{ill|HD212710|ja|HD212710|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/HD212710|qq=}}, a 5th magnitude star in {{ill|Cepheus|ja|ケフェウス座|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cepheus|qq=}}<ref group="note">Modern identifications were made using the star catalog data from ''Imperial Astronomical Treatise'' (欽定儀象考成) by Tsuchihashi, Chevalier, and Iida. See Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 297.</ref>.
== Worship and Rituals == The {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Big Dipper|qq=}} was seen as a celestial ladle pouring cosmic blessings. The ''{{ill|Records of the Grand Historian|ja|史記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Records of the Grand Historian|qq=}}'' describes the Big Dipper as revolving around the North Star, marking time, seasons, and ensuring the stability of the state<ref>''Shiji'' "Treatise on Astronomy" and ''Star Canon'' (星経), "The Big Dipper" section.</ref>.
The Heavenly Sovereign Emperor is associated with divine regalia, specifically a '''mirror''' and a '''sword'''. These items serve as both ritual implements and symbols of authority.
== Reading and Pronunciation == According to {{ill|Tetsuji Morohashi|ja|諸橋轍次|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tetsuji Morohashi|qq=}}’s ''{{ill|Dai Kan-Wa Jiten|ja|大漢和辞典|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dai Kan-Wa Jiten|qq=}}'' (1956), the historical reading of "天皇大帝" was ''Ten'wō Taitei'' (てんわうだいてい), pronounced as ''Tennō Daitei'' due to {{ill|Rendaku|ja|連声|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rendaku|qq=}}. As a constellation name, it was commonly read as ''Tenkō Taitei'' (てんこうたいてい)<ref>Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 173.</ref>. == See Also == * {{ill|North Star|ja|北極星|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/North Star|qq=}} * {{ill|Northern Celestial Pole|ja|北辰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Northern Celestial Pole|qq=}} * {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōken Bosatsu|qq=}} * [[:en:Ziwei_enclosure|purple forbidden enclosure]]

See {{ill|Mitsuo Toyama|ja|遠山美都男|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsuo Toyama|qq=}}, ''The Origins of the Emperor and Japan: Unraveling the Mystery of the "Great Kings of Asuka,"'' Kodansha Gendai Shinsho, 2003, pp. 271-272.</ref>. This coincided with the early use of the "Emperor" (天皇) title in Japan under {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=}}, though scholars debate which came first.
Taoism venerates the supreme deity as {{ill|Jade Emperor|ja|玉皇大帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jade Emperor|qq=}}, associating him with the [[:en:Ziwei_enclosure|purple forbidden enclosure]] and the {{ill|North Star|ja|北極星|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/North Star|qq=}}<ref group="note">Due to Earth’s precession, the celestial north pole shifted around 500 CE, transitioning from {{ill|Beta Ursae Minoris|lt=Kochab|ja|こぐま座ベータ星|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beta Ursae Minoris|qq=}} to {{ill|Polaris|ja|ポラリス (恒星)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Polaris|qq=}}.</ref>. The deity was identified as '''{{ill|Ziwei Emperor|lt=Beiji Ziwei Dadi|ja|北極紫微大帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ziwei Emperor|qq=}}'''. This belief later syncretized with the {{ill|Big Dipper Faith|ja|北斗信仰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Big Dipper Faith|qq=}}, deifying the Big Dipper as '''{{ill|Beidou Zhenjun|ja|北斗星君|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beidou Zhenjun|qq=}}'''. In time, it further merged with the Buddhist concept of {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōken Bosatsu|qq=}}, forming the '''Myōken faith'''. Consequently, "Beiji Ziwei Dadi" (North Pole Purple Tenuous Emperor) and "Beidou Zhenjun" (Big Dipper True Lord) are sometimes considered distinct and sometimes identified as the same deity.
The belief in the North Star's divine power extended to Chinese emperors and Japan’s imperial family. The robes of Chinese emperors featured the {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Big Dipper|qq=}} on the left sleeve and the {{ill|Zhinü|lt=Weaving Maiden|ja|織女|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhinü|qq=}}<ref group="note">This does not refer to {{ill|Vega|ja|ベガ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Vega|qq=}} alone but to the constellation {{ill|Weaving Maiden Three Stars|ja|織女三星|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Weaving Maiden Three Stars|qq=}}, which includes Vega.</ref> on the right. Meanwhile, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Agency|qq=}} preserves an imperial robe of {{ill|Emperor Komei|ja|孝明天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Komei|qq=}}, with the Big Dipper embroidered at the center of the upper back, though without the Weaving Maiden.
== The Heavenly Sovereign Emperor as a Constellation == [[File:和漢三才図絵「北極紫微垣之図」部分・上下逆.JPG|thumb|230px|right|''{{ill|Wakan Sansai Zue|ja|和漢三才図会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakan Sansai Zue|qq=}}'': "Map of the Purple Forbidden Enclosure" with ''Beiji Ziwei Dadi'' (北極紫微大帝) and ''Heavenly Sovereign Emperor'' (天皇大帝)]]
Beyond its religious significance, "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor" also referred to an actual asterism. It is mentioned in ''{{ill|Kaiyuan Zhanjing|ja|開元占経|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaiyuan Zhanjing|qq=}}'', Volume 69, "Ganshi's Central Observations" (甘氏中官占)<ref>Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 48.</ref>. In the ''{{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Jin|qq=}}'', ''Tianwen Zhi'' (Astronomical Records) states: "Within the 'mouth' is one star called 'Heavenly Sovereign Emperor'" (口中一星を天皇大帝と曰ふ).
This "mouth" refers to the asterism ''Gouchen'' (勾陳), which guarded the celestial palace of the Jade Emperor, the {{ill|Purple Forbidden Enclosure|ja|紫微垣|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Purple Forbidden Enclosure|qq=}}<ref>Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 153.</ref>. The star known as "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor" is believed to be {{ill|HD212710|ja|HD212710|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/HD212710|qq=}}, a 5th magnitude star in {{ill|Cepheus|ja|ケフェウス座|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cepheus|qq=}}<ref group="note">Modern identifications were made using the star catalog data from ''Imperial Astronomical Treatise'' (欽定儀象考成) by Tsuchihashi, Chevalier, and Iida. See Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 297.</ref>.
== Worship and Rituals == The {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Big Dipper|qq=}} was seen as a celestial ladle pouring cosmic blessings. The ''{{ill|Records of the Grand Historian|ja|史記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Records of the Grand Historian|qq=}}'' describes the Big Dipper as revolving around the North Star, marking time, seasons, and ensuring the stability of the state<ref>''Shiji'' "Treatise on Astronomy" and ''Star Canon'' (星経), "The Big Dipper" section.</ref>.
The Heavenly Sovereign Emperor is associated with divine regalia, specifically a '''mirror''' and a '''sword'''. These items serve as both ritual implements and symbols of authority.
== Reading and Pronunciation == According to {{ill|Tetsuji Morohashi|ja|諸橋轍次|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tetsuji Morohashi|qq=}}’s ''{{ill|Dai Kan-Wa Jiten|ja|大漢和辞典|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dai Kan-Wa Jiten|qq=}}'' (1956), the historical reading of "天皇大帝" was ''Ten'wō Taitei'' (てんわうだいてい), pronounced as ''Tennō Daitei'' due to {{ill|Rendaku|ja|連声|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rendaku|qq=}}. As a constellation name, it was commonly read as ''Tenkō Taitei'' (てんこうたいてい)<ref>Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 173.</ref>. == See Also == * {{ill|North Star|ja|北極星|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/North Star|qq=}} * {{ill|Northern Celestial Pole|ja|北辰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Northern Celestial Pole|qq=}} * {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōken Bosatsu|qq=}} * [[:en:Ziwei_enclosure|purple forbidden enclosure]]

{{draft categories| [[Category:Taoism]] [[Category:Chinese constellations]] [[Category:Four Sovereigns]] [[Category:C]][[Completion tier 2]][[Category:qq 1]] [[Category:Four heavenly ministers]] [[Category:Expanded pages from Wikipedia]] [[Category:c]][[Completion tier 2]][[Category:qq 2]] [[sn:Tianhuang Emperor]][[sn:Tianhuang Emperor]][[sn:Tianhuang Emperor]]}}
[[sn:Tianhuang Emperor]] [[ja:天皇大帝]] [[en:Tianhuang Emperor]]

{{for|The figure given the posthumous title of "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor"|{{ill|Liu Yan (Southern Han)|ja|劉龑|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Liu Yan (Southern Han)|qq=}}}} [[File:天皇大帝.JPG|thumb|Statue of the Heavenly Sovereign Emperor (Tianhuang Dadi) at Okayamajutenkyu, Taiwan]] [[File:Sterneamwalberla2.jpg|thumb|230px|right|Stars rotating around the North Star]] [[File:Draco constellation map.png|thumb|230px|right|Ursa Minor and Draco. The current North Star is marked as "Polaris"]]
'''Heavenly Sovereign Emperor''' (天皇大帝, ''Ten'nō Taitei''<ref>{{Cite book|author=Saburōsuke Tanabe|year=1991|title=Comprehensive Encyclopedia of Japanese Buddhist Statues, Volume 5: Syncretic Deities and Eminent Monks|page=18|publisher={{ill|Gyosei|ja|ぎょうせい|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyosei|qq=}}|isbn=4-324-02117-1}}</ref>) refers to the deification of the '''Northern Celestial Pole''' (北辰, ''Hokushin'')<ref>Hisao Fukushima, ''The Night Sky as Seen by Confucius,'' {{ill|Taishukan Shoten|ja|大修館書店|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishukan Shoten|qq=}}, 1997.</ref>. It is considered one of the most prominent theories regarding the origin of the title '''{{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor of Japan|qq=}}''' in Japan. It has sometimes been conflated with the [[Big Dipper]] due to the movement of the celestial pole.
[[Emperor Gaozong of Tang]] was named such and lived from 21 July 628<ref name="birth">According to Tang Taizong's biography in the ''[[Old Book of Tang]]'', Li Zhi was born on the ''gengyin'' day in the 6th month of the 2nd year of the ''Zhen'guan'' era of Taizong's reign. This corresponds to 21 Jul 628 in the Gregorian calendar. [(貞觀二年)六月庚寅，皇子治生] ''Old Book of Tang, vol. 2''</ref> – 27 December 683<ref name="death">Volume 203 of ''[[Zizhi Tongjian]]'' recorded that Gaozong died in the night of the ''dingsi'' day of the 12th month of the 1st year of the Hongdao era of his reign. This date corresponds to 27 Dec 683 in the Gregorian calendar. [(弘道元年)十二月，丁巳，...。是夜，....，上崩於貞觀殿。] ''Zizhi Tongjian'', vol.203.</ref>. The term Tenno (天皇) was first attested in 720 with the [[Nihon Shoki]] so this god or the title is attributed as a possible source of the title.
== Overview == Ancient China understood that stars appeared to rotate around a fixed point in the sky, which was called the '''Northern Celestial Pole''' (corresponding to the celestial north pole) and regarded as the '''center of the universe'''. This belief led to its deification and its incorporation into the titles used in {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taoism|qq=}} and later in Japan’s imperial system.
During the Tang dynasty, {{ill|Emperor Gaozong of Tang|ja|高宗 (唐)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Gaozong of Tang|qq=}} took the title of "Heavenly Sovereign" (天皇), and after his death, his consort {{ill|Wu Zetian|lt=Empress Wu Zetian|ja|則天武后|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wu Zetian|qq=}} granted him the posthumous title "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor" (天皇大帝)<ref>Gaozong’s use of the title "Heavenly Sovereign" was a "one-generation personal title" rather than a strict monarchic title. This occurred about a year and a half after {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=}} had begun using the title in Japan. See {{ill|Mitsuo Toyama|ja|遠山美都男|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsuo Toyama|qq=}}, ''The Origins of the Emperor and Japan: Unraveling the Mystery of the "Great Kings of Asuka,"'' Kodansha Gendai Shinsho, 2003, pp. 271-272.</ref>. This coincided with the early use of the "Emperor" (天皇) title in Japan under {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=}}, though scholars debate which came first.
Taoism venerates the supreme deity as {{ill|Jade Emperor|ja|玉皇大帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jade Emperor|qq=}}, associating him with the [[:en:Ziwei_enclosure|purple forbidden enclosure]] and the {{ill|North Star|ja|北極星|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/North Star|qq=}}<ref group="note">Due to Earth’s precession, the celestial north pole shifted around 500 CE, transitioning from {{ill|Beta Ursae Minoris|lt=Kochab|ja|こぐま座ベータ星|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beta Ursae Minoris|qq=}} to {{ill|Polaris|ja|ポラリス (恒星)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Polaris|qq=}}.</ref>. The deity was identified as '''{{ill|Ziwei Emperor|lt=Beiji Ziwei Dadi|ja|北極紫微大帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ziwei Emperor|qq=}}'''. This belief later syncretized with the {{ill|Big Dipper Faith|ja|北斗信仰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Big Dipper Faith|qq=}}, deifying the Big Dipper as '''{{ill|Beidou Zhenjun|ja|北斗星君|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beidou Zhenjun|qq=}}'''. In time, it further merged with the Buddhist concept of {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōken Bosatsu|qq=}}, forming the '''Myōken faith'''. Consequently, "Beiji Ziwei Dadi" (North Pole Purple Tenuous Emperor) and "Beidou Zhenjun" (Big Dipper True Lord) are sometimes considered distinct and sometimes identified as the same deity.
The belief in the North Star's divine power extended to Chinese emperors and Japan’s imperial family. The robes of Chinese emperors featured the {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Big Dipper|qq=}} on the left sleeve and the {{ill|Zhinü|lt=Weaving Maiden|ja|織女|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhinü|qq=}}<ref group="note">This does not refer to {{ill|Vega|ja|ベガ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Vega|qq=}} alone but to the constellation {{ill|Weaving Maiden Three Stars|ja|織女三星|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Weaving Maiden Three Stars|qq=}}, which includes Vega.</ref> on the right. Meanwhile, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Agency|qq=}} preserves an imperial robe of {{ill|Emperor Komei|ja|孝明天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Komei|qq=}}, with the Big Dipper embroidered at the center of the upper back, though without the Weaving Maiden.

This coincided with the early use of the "Emperor" (天皇) title in Japan under {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=}}, though scholars debate which came first.
Taoism venerates the supreme deity as {{ill|Jade Emperor|ja|玉皇大帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jade Emperor|qq=}}, associating him with the [[:en:Ziwei_enclosure|purple forbidden enclosure]] and the {{ill|North Star|ja|北極星|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/North Star|qq=}}<ref group="note">Due to Earth’s precession, the celestial north pole shifted around 500 CE, transitioning from {{ill|Beta Ursae Minoris|lt=Kochab|ja|こぐま座ベータ星|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beta Ursae Minoris|qq=}} to {{ill|Polaris|ja|ポラリス (恒星)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Polaris|qq=}}.</ref>. The deity was identified as '''{{ill|Ziwei Emperor|lt=Beiji Ziwei Dadi|ja|北極紫微大帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ziwei Emperor|qq=}}'''. This belief later syncretized with the {{ill|Big Dipper Faith|ja|北斗信仰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Big Dipper Faith|qq=}}, deifying the Big Dipper as '''{{ill|Beidou Zhenjun|ja|北斗星君|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beidou Zhenjun|qq=}}'''. In time, it further merged with the Buddhist concept of {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōken Bosatsu|qq=}}, forming the '''Myōken faith'''. Consequently, "Beiji Ziwei Dadi" (North Pole Purple Tenuous Emperor) and "Beidou Zhenjun" (Big Dipper True Lord) are sometimes considered distinct and sometimes identified as the same deity.
The belief in the North Star's divine power extended to Chinese emperors and Japan’s imperial family. The robes of Chinese emperors featured the {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Big Dipper|qq=}} on the left sleeve and the {{ill|Zhinü|lt=Weaving Maiden|ja|織女|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhinü|qq=}}<ref group="note">This does not refer to {{ill|Vega|ja|ベガ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Vega|qq=}} alone but to the constellation {{ill|Weaving Maiden Three Stars|ja|織女三星|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Weaving Maiden Three Stars|qq=}}, which includes Vega.</ref> on the right. Meanwhile, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Agency|qq=}} preserves an imperial robe of {{ill|Emperor Komei|ja|孝明天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Komei|qq=}}, with the Big Dipper embroidered at the center of the upper back, though without the Weaving Maiden.
== The Heavenly Sovereign Emperor as a Constellation == [[File:和漢三才図絵「北極紫微垣之図」部分・上下逆.JPG|thumb|230px|right|''{{ill|Wakan Sansai Zue|ja|和漢三才図会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakan Sansai Zue|qq=}}'': "Map of the Purple Forbidden Enclosure" with ''Beiji Ziwei Dadi'' (北極紫微大帝) and ''Heavenly Sovereign Emperor'' (天皇大帝)]]
Beyond its religious significance, "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor" also referred to an actual asterism. It is mentioned in ''{{ill|Kaiyuan Zhanjing|ja|開元占経|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaiyuan Zhanjing|qq=}}'', Volume 69, "Ganshi's Central Observations" (甘氏中官占)<ref>Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 48.</ref>. In the ''{{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Jin|qq=}}'', ''Tianwen Zhi'' (Astronomical Records) states: "Within the 'mouth' is one star called 'Heavenly Sovereign Emperor'" (口中一星を天皇大帝と曰ふ).
This "mouth" refers to the asterism ''Gouchen'' (勾陳), which guarded the celestial palace of the Jade Emperor, the {{ill|Purple Forbidden Enclosure|ja|紫微垣|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Purple Forbidden Enclosure|qq=}}<ref>Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 153.</ref>. The star known as "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor" is believed to be {{ill|HD212710|ja|HD212710|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/HD212710|qq=}}, a 5th magnitude star in {{ill|Cepheus|ja|ケフェウス座|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cepheus|qq=}}<ref group="note">Modern identifications were made using the star catalog data from ''Imperial Astronomical Treatise'' (欽定儀象考成) by Tsuchihashi, Chevalier, and Iida. See Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 297.</ref>.
== Worship and Rituals == The {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Big Dipper|qq=}} was seen as a celestial ladle pouring cosmic blessings. The ''{{ill|Records of the Grand Historian|ja|史記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Records of the Grand Historian|qq=}}'' describes the Big Dipper as revolving around the North Star, marking time, seasons, and ensuring the stability of the state<ref>''Shiji'' "Treatise on Astronomy" and ''Star Canon'' (星経), "The Big Dipper" section.</ref>.
The Heavenly Sovereign Emperor is associated with divine regalia, specifically a '''mirror''' and a '''sword'''. These items serve as both ritual implements and symbols of authority.
== Reading and Pronunciation == According to {{ill|Tetsuji Morohashi|ja|諸橋轍次|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tetsuji Morohashi|qq=}}’s ''{{ill|Dai Kan-Wa Jiten|ja|大漢和辞典|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dai Kan-Wa Jiten|qq=}}'' (1956), the historical reading of "天皇大帝" was ''Ten'wō Taitei'' (てんわうだいてい), pronounced as ''Tennō Daitei'' due to {{ill|Rendaku|ja|連声|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rendaku|qq=}}. As a constellation name, it was commonly read as ''Tenkō Taitei'' (てんこうたいてい)<ref>Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 173.</ref>. == See Also == * {{ill|North Star|ja|北極星|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/North Star|qq=}} * {{ill|Northern Celestial Pole|ja|北辰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Northern Celestial Pole|qq=}} * {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōken Bosatsu|qq=}} * [[:en:Ziwei_enclosure|purple forbidden enclosure]]
==Notes== <references group="note"/>

{{draft categories| [[Category:Taoism]] [[Category:Chinese constellations]] [[Category:Four Sovereigns]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:qq 1]] [[Category:Four heavenly ministers]] [[Category:Expanded pages from Wikipedia]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:qq 2]] [[sn:Tianhuang Emperor]][[sn:Tianhuang Emperor]][[sn:Tianhuang Emperor]]}}
[[sn:Tianhuang Emperor]] [[ja:天皇大帝]] [[en:Tianhuang Emperor]]

{{for|The figure given the posthumous title of "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor"|{{ill|Liu Yan (Southern Han)|ja|劉龑|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Liu Yan (Southern Han)|qq=}}}} [[File:天皇大帝.JPG|thumb|Statue of the Heavenly Sovereign Emperor (Tianhuang Dadi) at Okayamajutenkyu, Taiwan]] [[File:Sterneamwalberla2.jpg|thumb|230px|right|Stars rotating around the North Star]] [[File:Draco constellation map.png|thumb|230px|right|Ursa Minor and Draco. The current North Star is marked as "Polaris"]]
'''Heavenly Sovereign Emperor''' (天皇大帝, ''Ten'nō Taitei''<ref>{{Cite book|author=Saburōsuke Tanabe|year=1991|title=Comprehensive Encyclopedia of Japanese Buddhist Statues, Volume 5: Syncretic Deities and Eminent Monks|page=18|publisher={{ill|Gyosei|ja|ぎょうせい|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyosei|qq=}}|isbn=4-324-02117-1}}</ref>) refers to the deification of the '''Northern Celestial Pole''' (北辰, ''Hokushin'')<ref>Hisao Fukushima, ''The Night Sky as Seen by Confucius,'' {{ill|Taishukan Shoten|ja|大修館書店|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishukan Shoten|qq=}}, 1997.</ref>. It is considered one of the most prominent theories regarding the origin of the title '''{{ill|Emperor of Japan|ja|天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor of Japan|qq=}}''' in Japan. It has sometimes been conflated with the [[Big Dipper]] due to the movement of the celestial pole.
[[Emperor Gaozong of Tang]] was named such and lived from 21 July 628<ref name="birth">According to Tang Taizong's biography in the ''[[Old Book of Tang]]'', Li Zhi was born on the ''gengyin'' day in the 6th month of the 2nd year of the ''Zhen'guan'' era of Taizong's reign. This corresponds to 21 Jul 628 in the Gregorian calendar. [(貞觀二年)六月庚寅，皇子治生] ''Old Book of Tang, vol. 2''</ref> – 27 December 683<ref name="death">Volume 203 of ''[[Zizhi Tongjian]]'' recorded that Gaozong died in the night of the ''dingsi'' day of the 12th month of the 1st year of the Hongdao era of his reign. This date corresponds to 27 Dec 683 in the Gregorian calendar. [(弘道元年)十二月，丁巳，...。是夜，....，上崩於貞觀殿。] ''Zizhi Tongjian'', vol.203.</ref>. The term Tenno (天皇) was first attested in 720 with the [[Nihon Shoki]] so this god or the title is attributed as a possible source of the title.
== Overview == Ancient China understood that stars appeared to rotate around a fixed point in the sky, which was called the '''Northern Celestial Pole''' (corresponding to the celestial north pole) and regarded as the '''center of the universe'''. This belief led to its deification and its incorporation into the titles used in {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taoism|qq=}} and later in Japan’s imperial system.
During the Tang dynasty, {{ill|Emperor Gaozong of Tang|ja|高宗 (唐)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Gaozong of Tang|qq=}} took the title of "Heavenly Sovereign" (天皇), and after his death, his consort {{ill|Wu Zetian|lt=Empress Wu Zetian|ja|則天武后|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wu Zetian|qq=}} granted him the posthumous title "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor" (天皇大帝)<ref>Gaozong’s use of the title "Heavenly Sovereign" was a "one-generation personal title" rather than a strict monarchic title. This occurred about a year and a half after {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=}} had begun using the title in Japan. See {{ill|Mitsuo Toyama|ja|遠山美都男|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsuo Toyama|qq=}}, ''The Origins of the Emperor and Japan: Unraveling the Mystery of the "Great Kings of Asuka,"'' Kodansha Gendai Shinsho, 2003, pp. 271-272.</ref>. This coincided with the early use of the "Emperor" (天皇) title in Japan under {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=}}, though scholars debate which came first.
Taoism venerates the supreme deity as {{ill|Jade Emperor|ja|玉皇大帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jade Emperor|qq=}}, associating him with the [[:en:Ziwei_enclosure|purple forbidden enclosure]] and the {{ill|North Star|ja|北極星|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/North Star|qq=}}<ref group="note">Due to Earth’s precession, the celestial north pole shifted around 500 CE, transitioning from {{ill|Beta Ursae Minoris|lt=Kochab|ja|こぐま座ベータ星|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beta Ursae Minoris|qq=}} to {{ill|Polaris|ja|ポラリス (恒星)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Polaris|qq=}}.</ref>. The deity was identified as '''{{ill|Ziwei Emperor|lt=Beiji Ziwei Dadi|ja|北極紫微大帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ziwei Emperor|qq=}}'''. This belief later syncretized with the {{ill|Big Dipper Faith|ja|北斗信仰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Big Dipper Faith|qq=}}, deifying the Big Dipper as '''{{ill|Beidou Zhenjun|ja|北斗星君|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beidou Zhenjun|qq=}}'''. In time, it further merged with the Buddhist concept of {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōken Bosatsu|qq=}}, forming the '''Myōken faith'''. Consequently, "Beiji Ziwei Dadi" (North Pole Purple Tenuous Emperor) and "Beidou Zhenjun" (Big Dipper True Lord) are sometimes considered distinct and sometimes identified as the same deity.
The belief in the North Star's divine power extended to Chinese emperors and Japan’s imperial family. The robes of Chinese emperors featured the {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Big Dipper|qq=}} on the left sleeve and the {{ill|Zhinü|lt=Weaving Maiden|ja|織女|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhinü|qq=}}<ref group="note">This does not refer to {{ill|Vega|ja|ベガ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Vega|qq=}} alone but to the constellation {{ill|Weaving Maiden Three Stars|ja|織女三星|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Weaving Maiden Three Stars|qq=}}, which includes Vega.</ref> on the right. Meanwhile, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Agency|qq=}} preserves an imperial robe of {{ill|Emperor Komei|ja|孝明天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Komei|qq=}}, with the Big Dipper embroidered at the center of the upper back, though without the Weaving Maiden.
== The Heavenly Sovereign Emperor as a Constellation == [[File:和漢三才図絵「北極紫微垣之図」部分・上下逆.JPG|thumb|230px|right|''{{ill|Wakan Sansai Zue|ja|和漢三才図会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakan Sansai Zue|qq=}}'': "Map of the Purple Forbidden Enclosure" with ''Beiji Ziwei Dadi'' (北極紫微大帝) and ''Heavenly Sovereign Emperor'' (天皇大帝)]]
Beyond its religious significance, "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor" also referred to an actual asterism. It is mentioned in ''{{ill|Kaiyuan Zhanjing|ja|開元占経|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaiyuan Zhanjing|qq=}}'', Volume 69, "Ganshi's Central Observations" (甘氏中官占)<ref>Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 48.</ref>. In the ''{{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Jin|qq=}}'', ''Tianwen Zhi'' (Astronomical Records) states: "Within the 'mouth' is one star called 'Heavenly Sovereign Emperor'" (口中一星を天皇大帝と曰ふ).
This "mouth" refers to the asterism ''Gouchen'' (勾陳), which guarded the celestial palace of the Jade Emperor, the {{ill|Purple Forbidden Enclosure|ja|紫微垣|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Purple Forbidden Enclosure|qq=}}<ref>Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 153.</ref>. The star known as "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor" is believed to be {{ill|HD212710|ja|HD212710|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/HD212710|qq=}}, a 5th magnitude star in {{ill|Cepheus|ja|ケフェウス座|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cepheus|qq=}}<ref group="note">Modern identifications were made using the star catalog data from ''Imperial Astronomical Treatise'' (欽定儀象考成) by Tsuchihashi, Chevalier, and Iida. See Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 297.</ref>.
== Worship and Rituals == The {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Big Dipper|qq=}} was seen as a celestial ladle pouring cosmic blessings. The ''{{ill|Records of the Grand Historian|ja|史記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Records of the Grand Historian|qq=}}'' describes the Big Dipper as revolving around the North Star, marking time, seasons, and ensuring the stability of the state<ref>''Shiji'' "Treatise on Astronomy" and ''Star Canon'' (星経), "The Big Dipper" section.</ref>.
The Heavenly Sovereign Emperor is associated with divine regalia, specifically a '''mirror''' and a '''sword'''. These items serve as both ritual implements and symbols of authority.

As a constellation name, it was commonly read as ''Tenkō Taitei'' (てんこうたいてい)<ref>Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 173.</ref>. == See Also == * {{ill|North Star|ja|北極星|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/North Star|qq=}} * {{ill|Northern Celestial Pole|ja|北辰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Northern Celestial Pole|qq=}} * {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōken Bosatsu|qq=}} * [[:en:Ziwei_enclosure|purple forbidden enclosure]]

The current North Star is marked as "Polaris"]]
'''Heavenly Sovereign Emperor''' (天皇大帝, ''Ten'nō Taitei''<ref>{{Cite book|author=Saburōsuke Tanabe|year=1991|title=Comprehensive Encyclopedia of Japanese Buddhist Statues, Volume 5: Syncretic Deities and Eminent Monks|page=18|publisher={{ill|Gyosei|ja|ぎょうせい|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyosei|qq=}}|isbn=4-324-02117-1}}</ref>) refers to the deification of the '''Northern Celestial Pole''' (北辰, ''Hokushin'')<ref>Hisao Fukushima, ''The Night Sky as Seen by Confucius,'' {{ill|Taishukan Shoten|ja|大修館書店|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishukan Shoten|qq=}}, 1997.</ref>. It has sometimes been conflated with the [[Big Dipper]] due to the movement of the celestial pole.

This corresponds to 21 Jul 628 in the Gregorian calendar. [(貞觀二年)六月庚寅，皇子治生] ''Old Book of Tang, vol. 2''</ref> – 27 December 683<ref name="death">Volume 203 of ''[[Zizhi Tongjian]]'' recorded that Gaozong died in the night of the ''dingsi'' day of the 12th month of the 1st year of the Hongdao era of his reign. This date corresponds to 27 Dec 683 in the Gregorian calendar. [(弘道元年)十二月，丁巳，...。是夜，....，上崩於貞觀殿。] ''Zizhi Tongjian'', vol.203.</ref>. This belief led to its deification and its incorporation into the titles used in {{ill|Taoism|ja|道教|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taoism|qq=}} and later in Japan’s imperial system.
During the Tang dynasty, {{ill|Emperor Gaozong of Tang|ja|高宗 (唐)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Gaozong of Tang|qq=}} took the title of "Heavenly Sovereign" (天皇), and after his death, his consort {{ill|Wu Zetian|lt=Empress Wu Zetian|ja|則天武后|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wu Zetian|qq=}} granted him the posthumous title "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor" (天皇大帝)<ref>Gaozong’s use of the title "Heavenly Sovereign" was a "one-generation personal title" rather than a strict monarchic title. This occurred about a year and a half after {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=}} had begun using the title in Japan. See {{ill|Mitsuo Toyama|ja|遠山美都男|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsuo Toyama|qq=}}, ''The Origins of the Emperor and Japan: Unraveling the Mystery of the "Great Kings of Asuka,"'' Kodansha Gendai Shinsho, 2003, pp. 271-272.</ref>. This coincided with the early use of the "Emperor" (天皇) title in Japan under {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=}}, though scholars debate which came first.
Taoism venerates the supreme deity as {{ill|Jade Emperor|ja|玉皇大帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jade Emperor|qq=}}, associating him with the [[:en:Ziwei_enclosure|purple forbidden enclosure]] and the {{ill|North Star|ja|北極星|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/North Star|qq=}}<ref group="note">Due to Earth’s precession, the celestial north pole shifted around 500 CE, transitioning from {{ill|Beta Ursae Minoris|lt=Kochab|ja|こぐま座ベータ星|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beta Ursae Minoris|qq=}} to {{ill|Polaris|ja|ポラリス (恒星)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Polaris|qq=}}.</ref>. The deity was identified as '''{{ill|Ziwei Emperor|lt=Beiji Ziwei Dadi|ja|北極紫微大帝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ziwei Emperor|qq=}}'''. This belief later syncretized with the {{ill|Big Dipper Faith|ja|北斗信仰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Big Dipper Faith|qq=}}, deifying the Big Dipper as '''{{ill|Beidou Zhenjun|ja|北斗星君|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beidou Zhenjun|qq=}}'''. In time, it further merged with the Buddhist concept of {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōken Bosatsu|qq=}}, forming the '''Myōken faith'''. Consequently, "Beiji Ziwei Dadi" (North Pole Purple Tenuous Emperor) and "Beidou Zhenjun" (Big Dipper True Lord) are sometimes considered distinct and sometimes identified as the same deity.
The belief in the North Star's divine power extended to Chinese emperors and Japan’s imperial family. The robes of Chinese emperors featured the {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Big Dipper|qq=}} on the left sleeve and the {{ill|Zhinü|lt=Weaving Maiden|ja|織女|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhinü|qq=}}<ref group="note">This does not refer to {{ill|Vega|ja|ベガ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Vega|qq=}} alone but to the constellation {{ill|Weaving Maiden Three Stars|ja|織女三星|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Weaving Maiden Three Stars|qq=}}, which includes Vega.</ref> on the right. Meanwhile, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Agency|qq=}} preserves an imperial robe of {{ill|Emperor Komei|ja|孝明天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Komei|qq=}}, with the Big Dipper embroidered at the center of the upper back, though without the Weaving Maiden.
== The Heavenly Sovereign Emperor as a Constellation == [[File:和漢三才図絵「北極紫微垣之図」部分・上下逆.JPG|thumb|230px|right|''{{ill|Wakan Sansai Zue|ja|和漢三才図会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakan Sansai Zue|qq=}}'': "Map of the Purple Forbidden Enclosure" with ''Beiji Ziwei Dadi'' (北極紫微大帝) and ''Heavenly Sovereign Emperor'' (天皇大帝)]]
Beyond its religious significance, "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor" also referred to an actual asterism. It is mentioned in ''{{ill|Kaiyuan Zhanjing|ja|開元占経|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaiyuan Zhanjing|qq=}}'', Volume 69, "Ganshi's Central Observations" (甘氏中官占)<ref>Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 48.</ref>. In the ''{{ill|Book of Jin|ja|晋書|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Jin|qq=}}'', ''Tianwen Zhi'' (Astronomical Records) states: "Within the 'mouth' is one star called 'Heavenly Sovereign Emperor'" (口中一星を天皇大帝と曰ふ).
This "mouth" refers to the asterism ''Gouchen'' (勾陳), which guarded the celestial palace of the Jade Emperor, the {{ill|Purple Forbidden Enclosure|ja|紫微垣|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Purple Forbidden Enclosure|qq=}}<ref>Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 153.</ref>. The star known as "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor" is believed to be {{ill|HD212710|ja|HD212710|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/HD212710|qq=}}, a 5th magnitude star in {{ill|Cepheus|ja|ケフェウス座|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cepheus|qq=}}<ref group="note">Modern identifications were made using the star catalog data from ''Imperial Astronomical Treatise'' (欽定儀象考成) by Tsuchihashi, Chevalier, and Iida. See Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 297.</ref>.
== Worship and Rituals == The {{ill|Big Dipper|ja|北斗七星|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Big Dipper|qq=}} was seen as a celestial ladle pouring cosmic blessings. The ''{{ill|Records of the Grand Historian|ja|史記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Records of the Grand Historian|qq=}}'' describes the Big Dipper as revolving around the North Star, marking time, seasons, and ensuring the stability of the state<ref>''Shiji'' "Treatise on Astronomy" and ''Star Canon'' (星経), "The Big Dipper" section.</ref>.
The Heavenly Sovereign Emperor is associated with divine regalia, specifically a '''mirror''' and a '''sword'''. These items serve as both ritual implements and symbols of authority.
== Reading and Pronunciation == According to {{ill|Tetsuji Morohashi|ja|諸橋轍次|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tetsuji Morohashi|qq=}}’s ''{{ill|Dai Kan-Wa Jiten|ja|大漢和辞典|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dai Kan-Wa Jiten|qq=}}'' (1956), the historical reading of "天皇大帝" was ''Ten'wō Taitei'' (てんわうだいてい), pronounced as ''Tennō Daitei'' due to {{ill|Rendaku|ja|連声|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rendaku|qq=}}. As a constellation name, it was commonly read as ''Tenkō Taitei'' (てんこうたいてい)<ref>Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 173.</ref>. == See Also == * {{ill|North Star|ja|北極星|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/North Star|qq=}} * {{ill|Northern Celestial Pole|ja|北辰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Northern Celestial Pole|qq=}} * {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōken Bosatsu|qq=}} * [[:en:Ziwei_enclosure|purple forbidden enclosure]]
==Notes== <references group="note"/>

[[sn:Tianhuang Emperor]] [[ja:天皇大帝]] [[en:Tianhuang Emperor]]

revision.text:
{{for|The figure given the posthumous title of "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor"|[[:en:Liu Yan (emperor)|Liu Yan (Southern Han)]]}} [[File:天皇大帝.JPG|thumb|Statue of the Heavenly Sovereign Emperor (Tianhuang Dadi) at Okayamajutenkyu, Taiwan]] [[File:Sterneamwalberla2.jpg|thumb|230px|right|Stars rotating around the North Star]] [[File:Draco constellation map.png|thumb|230px|right|Ursa Minor and Draco. The current North Star is marked as "Polaris"]]
'''Heavenly Sovereign Emperor''' (天皇大帝, ''Ten'nō Taitei''<ref>{{Cite book|author=Saburōsuke Tanabe|year=1991|title=Comprehensive Encyclopedia of Japanese Buddhist Statues, Volume 5: Syncretic Deities and Eminent Monks|page=18|publisher={{ill|Gyosei|ja|ぎょうせい|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyosei|qq=}}|isbn=4-324-02117-1}}</ref>) refers to the deification of the '''Northern Celestial Pole''' (北辰, ''Hokushin'')<ref>Hisao Fukushima, ''The Night Sky as Seen by Confucius,'' {{ill|Taishukan Shoten|ja|大修館書店|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishukan Shoten|qq=}}, 1997.</ref>. It is considered one of the most prominent theories regarding the origin of the title '''[[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]]''' in Japan. It has sometimes been conflated with the [[Big Dipper]] due to the movement of the celestial pole.
[[Emperor Gaozong of Tang]] was named such and lived from 21 July 628<ref name="birth">According to Tang Taizong's biography in the ''[[Old Book of Tang]]'', Li Zhi was born on the ''gengyin'' day in the 6th month of the 2nd year of the ''Zhen'guan'' era of Taizong's reign. This corresponds to 21 Jul 628 in the Gregorian calendar. [(貞觀二年)六月庚寅，皇子治生] ''Old Book of Tang, vol. 2''</ref> – 27 December 683<ref name="death">Volume 203 of ''[[Zizhi Tongjian]]'' recorded that Gaozong died in the night of the ''dingsi'' day of the 12th month of the 1st year of the Hongdao era of his reign. This date corresponds to 27 Dec 683 in the Gregorian calendar. [(弘道元年)十二月，丁巳，...。是夜，....，上崩於貞觀殿。] ''Zizhi Tongjian'', vol.203.</ref>. The term Tenno (天皇) was first attested in 720 with the [[Nihon Shoki]] so this god or the title is attributed as a possible source of the title.
== Overview == Ancient China understood that stars appeared to rotate around a fixed point in the sky, which was called the '''Northern Celestial Pole''' (corresponding to the celestial north pole) and regarded as the '''center of the universe'''. This belief led to its deification and its incorporation into the titles used in [[:en:Taoism|Taoism]] and later in Japan’s imperial system.
During the Tang dynasty, [[:en:Emperor Gaozong of Tang|Emperor Gaozong of Tang]] took the title of "Heavenly Sovereign" (天皇), and after his death, his consort {{ill|Wu Zetian|lt=Empress Wu Zetian|ja|則天武后|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wu Zetian|qq=}} granted him the posthumous title "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor" (天皇大帝)<ref>Gaozong’s use of the title "Heavenly Sovereign" was a "one-generation personal title" rather than a strict monarchic title. This occurred about a year and a half after {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=}} had begun using the title in Japan. See {{ill|Mitsuo Toyama|ja|遠山美都男|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsuo Toyama|qq=}}, ''The Origins of the Emperor and Japan: Unraveling the Mystery of the "Great Kings of Asuka,"'' Kodansha Gendai Shinsho, 2003, pp. 271-272.</ref>. This coincided with the early use of the "Emperor" (天皇) title in Japan under {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=}}, though scholars debate which came first.
Taoism venerates the supreme deity as [[:en:Jade Emperor|Jade Emperor]], associating him with the [[:en:Ziwei_enclosure|purple forbidden enclosure]] and the {{ill|North Star|ja|北極星|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/North Star|qq=}}<ref group="note">Due to Earth’s precession, the celestial north pole shifted around 500 CE, transitioning from [[:en:Beta Ursae Minoris|Kochab]] to [[:en:Polaris|Polaris]].</ref>. The deity was identified as '''[[:en:Ziwei Emperor|Beiji Ziwei Dadi]]'''. This belief later syncretized with the {{ill|Big Dipper Faith|ja|北斗信仰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Big Dipper Faith|qq=}}, deifying the Big Dipper as '''{{ill|Beidou Zhenjun|ja|北斗星君|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beidou Zhenjun|qq=}}'''. In time, it further merged with the Buddhist concept of [[:en:Myōken|Myōken Bosatsu]], forming the '''Myōken faith'''. Consequently, "Beiji Ziwei Dadi" (North Pole Purple Tenuous Emperor) and "Beidou Zhenjun" (Big Dipper True Lord) are sometimes considered distinct and sometimes identified as the same deity.
The belief in the North Star's divine power extended to Chinese emperors and Japan’s imperial family. The robes of Chinese emperors featured the [[:en:Big Dipper|Big Dipper]] on the left sleeve and the {{ill|Zhinü|lt=Weaving Maiden|ja|織女|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhinü|qq=}}<ref group="note">This does not refer to [[:en:Vega|Vega]] alone but to the constellation {{ill|Weaving Maiden Three Stars|ja|織女三星|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Weaving Maiden Three Stars|qq=}}, which includes Vega.</ref> on the right. Meanwhile, the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] preserves an imperial robe of [[:en:Emperor Kōmei|Emperor Komei]], with the Big Dipper embroidered at the center of the upper back, though without the Weaving Maiden.
== The Heavenly Sovereign Emperor as a Constellation == [[File:和漢三才図絵「北極紫微垣之図」部分・上下逆.JPG|thumb|230px|right|''[[:en:Wakan Sansai Zue|Wakan Sansai Zue]]'': "Map of the Purple Forbidden Enclosure" with ''Beiji Ziwei Dadi'' (北極紫微大帝) and ''Heavenly Sovereign Emperor'' (天皇大帝)]]
Beyond its religious significance, "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor" also referred to an actual asterism. It is mentioned in ''{{ill|Kaiyuan Zhanjing|ja|開元占経|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaiyuan Zhanjing|qq=}}'', Volume 69, "Ganshi's Central Observations" (甘氏中官占)<ref>Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 48.</ref>. In the ''[[:en:Book of Jin|Book of Jin]]'', ''Tianwen Zhi'' (Astronomical Records) states: "Within the 'mouth' is one star called 'Heavenly Sovereign Emperor'" (口中一星を天皇大帝と曰ふ).
This "mouth" refers to the asterism ''Gouchen'' (勾陳), which guarded the celestial palace of the Jade Emperor, the [[:en:Ziwei enclosure|Purple Forbidden Enclosure]]<ref>Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 153.</ref>. The star known as "Heavenly Sovereign Emperor" is believed to be {{ill|HD212710|ja|HD212710|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/HD212710|qq=}}, a 5th magnitude star in [[:en:Cepheus (constellation)|Cepheus]]<ref group="note">Modern identifications were made using the star catalog data from ''Imperial Astronomical Treatise'' (欽定儀象考成) by Tsuchihashi, Chevalier, and Iida. See Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 297.</ref>.
== Worship and Rituals == The [[:en:Big Dipper|Big Dipper]] was seen as a celestial ladle pouring cosmic blessings. The ''[[:en:Shiji|Records of the Grand Historian]]'' describes the Big Dipper as revolving around the North Star, marking time, seasons, and ensuring the stability of the state<ref>''Shiji'' "Treatise on Astronomy" and ''Star Canon'' (星経), "The Big Dipper" section.</ref>.
The Heavenly Sovereign Emperor is associated with divine regalia, specifically a '''mirror''' and a '''sword'''. These items serve as both ritual implements and symbols of authority.
== Reading and Pronunciation == According to [[:en:Tetsuji Morohashi|Tetsuji Morohashi]]’s ''[[:en:Dai Kan-Wa Jiten|Dai Kan-Wa Jiten]]'' (1956), the historical reading of "天皇大帝" was ''Ten'wō Taitei'' (てんわうだいてい), pronounced as ''Tennō Daitei'' due to {{ill|Rendaku|ja|連声|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rendaku|qq=}}. As a constellation name, it was commonly read as ''Tenkō Taitei'' (てんこうたいてい)<ref>Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 173.</ref>. == See Also == * {{ill|North Star|ja|北極星|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/North Star|qq=}} * {{ill|Northern Celestial Pole|ja|北辰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Northern Celestial Pole|qq=}} * [[:en:Myōken|Myōken Bosatsu]] * [[:en:Ziwei_enclosure|purple forbidden enclosure]]
==Notes== <references group="note"/>
==References==

It is mentioned in ''{{ill|Kaiyuan Zhanjing|ja|開元占経|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaiyuan Zhanjing|qq=}}'', Volume 69, "Ganshi's Central Observations" (甘氏中官占)<ref>Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 48.</ref>. See Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 297.</ref>.
== Worship and Rituals == The [[:en:Big Dipper|Big Dipper]] was seen as a celestial ladle pouring cosmic blessings. The ''[[:en:Shiji|Records of the Grand Historian]]'' describes the Big Dipper as revolving around the North Star, marking time, seasons, and ensuring the stability of the state<ref>''Shiji'' "Treatise on Astronomy" and ''Star Canon'' (星経), "The Big Dipper" section.</ref>.
The Heavenly Sovereign Emperor is associated with divine regalia, specifically a '''mirror''' and a '''sword'''. These items serve as both ritual implements and symbols of authority.
== Reading and Pronunciation == According to [[:en:Tetsuji Morohashi|Tetsuji Morohashi]]’s ''[[:en:Dai Kan-Wa Jiten|Dai Kan-Wa Jiten]]'' (1956), the historical reading of "天皇大帝" was ''Ten'wō Taitei'' (てんわうだいてい), pronounced as ''Tennō Daitei'' due to {{ill|Rendaku|ja|連声|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rendaku|qq=}}. As a constellation name, it was commonly read as ''Tenkō Taitei'' (てんこうたいてい)<ref>Masatsugu Osaki (1987), p. 173.</ref>. == See Also == * {{ill|North Star|ja|北極星|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/North Star|qq=}} * {{ill|Northern Celestial Pole|ja|北辰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Northern Celestial Pole|qq=}} * [[:en:Myōken|Myōken Bosatsu]] * [[:en:Ziwei_enclosure|purple forbidden enclosure]]
==Notes== <references group="note"/>
==References==
